{{Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamatohime-no-miya, shaden-2.jpg|256px|Yamatohime-no-miya]] | name=Yamatohime-no-miya | location=[[Mie Prefecture]], [[Ise City]], Kusube-cho, Akaidani 5[http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/shrine/area09.php "Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan | 125 Shrines 5"] (Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan, accessed May 3, 2015) | deity=[[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] | Rank=Betto Shrine of the [[Kōdaijingū|Inner Shrine]] | Foundation=[[1923]] (Taishō 12) | Main Structure=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates={{Coord|34|29|9.2|N|136|43|22.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Festival= }} '''Yamatohime-no-miya''' (倭姫宮) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Ise City]], Kusube-cho. It is a subsidiary shrine ("betsugu") of the [[Kōtai Jingu (Inner Shrine)|Inner Shrine]] of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]].
== Overview == [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_02.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii gate at the Omotesando entrance]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_03.JPG|thumb|200px|Chōzuya (purification fountain)]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_04.JPG|thumb|200px|Stone steps along the approach]]
In [[Ise Jingu]], there are 14 [[Betsugu]] (associated shrines) in addition to the [[Inner Shrine]] (Naiku) and the [[Outer Shrine]] (Geku). These include {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月讀宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|2=es|3=Tsukiyomi no miya (Naikū)|4=ja|5=月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tsukiyominomiya|ja|月夜見宮|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Izawa-no-Miya|ja|伊雑宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kazebi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|12=draft}}, and others. Among them, the creation date is clearly documented only for Yamatohime-no-Miya (倭姫宮), which was established on [[November 5, 1923]] (Taisho 12), making it the newest of the shrines.
The enshrined deity is {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|12=draft}}, the fourth imperial princess of the 11th emperor, [[Emperor Suinin]], as told in [[Japanese mythology]].
Like other outlying Betsugu shrines, the site has a Shukueiya (宿衛屋), a lodging for [[Shinto priest]]s who are stationed during visiting hours. They manage the distribution of talismans and amulets, as well as arrangements for [[kagura]] and ritual offerings.
=== location === Adjacent to Kuratayama (倉田山) lies "Ainoyama" (間の山), home to a small [[kofun]] (ancient tomb) called Onoue-no-Goryo (尾上御陵). This tomb is traditionally believed to be the burial site of Yamatohime-no-Mikoto. In honor of this connection, the temple town at the base of 常明寺 (Jōmyō-ji) was renamed '''Yamato Town''' (倭町, Yamatomachi) in [[1868]] (Meiji 1). There are conflicting theories about the tomb's shape, with some identifying it as a circular tomb ([[enzuka]]) and others as a keyhole-shaped mound ([[zenpō-kōen-fun]]).
In [[1886]] (Meiji 19), the residents of Ujiyamada Town formed the {{ill|Shin’enkai|ja|神苑会|12=draft}}, a [[foundation]] focused on preventing fires from spreading to the shrine and preserving the shrine's aesthetic and cleanliness. In [[1891]] (Meiji 24), the Shin’enkai established the '''Jingu Agricultural Museum''' (神宮農業館), Japan’s oldest [[industrial museum]], near the Outer Shrine. In [[1905]] (Meiji 38), the museum was relocated to Kuratayama.
During [[Emperor Meiji]]'s [[Imperial visit]] to Ise Jingu, road improvements were necessary to accommodate [[automobiles]]. A road passing through Kuratayama was completed in [[1910]] (Meiji 43) and named '''Gokō-dōri''' (御幸通り, Imperial Road). The following year, in [[1911]] (Meiji 44), the Shin’enkai completed the {{ill|Jingu Chokokan|ja|神宮徴古館|12=draft}}, which featured a Versailles-inspired front garden. The Chokokan included a state guest room used for resting by dignitaries such as [[Emperor Taisho]]. Further development in Kuratayama as an educational district followed the relocation of Jingu Kōgakkan (now {{ill|Kōgakkan University|ja|皇學館大学|12=draft}}) to the area in [[1919]] (Taisho 8).
Due to the proximity of Onoue-no-Goryo, Yamatohime-no-Miya was constructed on a cleared section of Kuratayama. In [[1928]] (Showa 3), the tomb was officially designated as the '''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Burial Reference Site''' (倭姫命御陵墓参考地) and placed under the management of the [[Imperial Household Agency]]. == History == [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] is credited with establishing the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], creating rituals, and setting the foundation of [[Shinto]] practices. However, no shrine dedicated to her existed before the Meiji period.
Calls for a shrine arose around 1887 (Meiji 20), culminating in its official approval in 1912 (Taishō 1). The shrine was founded as a subsidiary of the Inner Shrine in 1923 (Taishō 12), following national approval in 1919 (Taishō 8).
During the 1945 (Shōwa 20) Ujiyamada air raids, the shukueiya was burned, but the main structure remained intact.
== deity == *Main deity: [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] (やまとひめのみこと)
== Grounds ==
Main Hall: Modeled after the Inner Shrine with [[Chigi]] and [[Katsuogi]], featuring a [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architectural style with thatched roofing and facing south.
Shukueiya: Provides blessings and talismans.
== Festivals == Festivals follow the Inner Shrine’s schedule, including:
[[Saitan-sai]] (January 1): Celebrating the new year and imperial lineage.
[[Kigensetsu]] (February 11): Commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s founding of the nation.
[[Kinen-sai]] (February 20): Praying for bountiful harvests.
[[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (October 24-25): Offering the year’s harvest to Amaterasu.
[[Niinamesai]] (November 26): Thanking the gods for the harvest.
== Worshipers == The Yamato-hime Shrine Reverence Association, founded in 1948 (Shōwa 23), holds regular events, including biannual festivals in May and November.
== Access == *Nearest station: [[Kintetsu]] [[Toba Line]], [[Isuzugawa Station]], 10 minutes on foot. *Parking: Available at the adjacent Jingu Chōkokan museum.
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also ==
* [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] * [[Ise Grand Shrine]] * [[List of 125 Shrines of Ise]] * [[Moto-Ise Shrines]]

{{Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamatohime-no-miya, shaden-2.jpg|256px|Yamatohime-no-miya]] | name=Yamatohime-no-miya | location=[[Mie Prefecture]], [[Ise City]], Kusube-cho, Akaidani 5[http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/shrine/area09.php "Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan | 125 Shrines 5"] (Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan, accessed May 3, 2015) | deity=[[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] | Rank=Betto Shrine of the [[Kōdaijingū|Inner Shrine]] | Foundation=[[1923]] (Taishō 12) | Main Structure=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates={{Coord|34|29|9.2|N|136|43|22.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Festival= }} '''Yamatohime-no-miya''' (倭姫宮) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Ise City]], Kusube-cho. It is a subsidiary shrine ("betsugu") of the [[Kōtai Jingu (Inner Shrine)|Inner Shrine]] of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]].
== Overview == [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_02.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii gate at the Omotesando entrance]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_03.JPG|thumb|200px|Chōzuya (purification fountain)]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_04.JPG|thumb|200px|Stone steps along the approach]]
In [[Ise Jingu]], there are 14 [[Betsugu]] (associated shrines) in addition to the [[Inner Shrine]] (Naiku) and the [[Outer Shrine]] (Geku). These include {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月讀宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|2=es|3=Tsukiyomi no miya (Naikū)|4=ja|5=月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tsukiyominomiya|ja|月夜見宮|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Izawa-no-Miya|ja|伊雑宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kazebi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, and others. Among them, the creation date is clearly documented only for Yamatohime-no-Miya (倭姫宮), which was established on [[November 5, 1923]] (Taisho 12), making it the newest of the shrines.
The enshrined deity is {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|12=draft|12=draft}}, the fourth imperial princess of the 11th emperor, [[Emperor Suinin]], as told in [[Japanese mythology]].
Like other outlying Betsugu shrines, the site has a Shukueiya (宿衛屋), a lodging for [[Shinto priest]]s who are stationed during visiting hours. They manage the distribution of talismans and amulets, as well as arrangements for [[kagura]] and ritual offerings.
=== location === Adjacent to Kuratayama (倉田山) lies "Ainoyama" (間の山), home to a small [[kofun]] (ancient tomb) called Onoue-no-Goryo (尾上御陵). This tomb is traditionally believed to be the burial site of Yamatohime-no-Mikoto. In honor of this connection, the temple town at the base of 常明寺 (Jōmyō-ji) was renamed '''Yamato Town''' (倭町, Yamatomachi) in [[1868]] (Meiji 1). There are conflicting theories about the tomb's shape, with some identifying it as a circular tomb ([[enzuka]]) and others as a keyhole-shaped mound ([[zenpō-kōen-fun]]).
In [[1886]] (Meiji 19), the residents of Ujiyamada Town formed the {{ill|Shin’enkai|ja|神苑会|12=draft|12=draft}}, a [[foundation]] focused on preventing fires from spreading to the shrine and preserving the shrine's aesthetic and cleanliness. In [[1891]] (Meiji 24), the Shin’enkai established the '''Jingu Agricultural Museum''' (神宮農業館), Japan’s oldest [[industrial museum]], near the Outer Shrine. In [[1905]] (Meiji 38), the museum was relocated to Kuratayama.
During [[Emperor Meiji]]'s [[Imperial visit]] to Ise Jingu, road improvements were necessary to accommodate [[automobiles]]. A road passing through Kuratayama was completed in [[1910]] (Meiji 43) and named '''Gokō-dōri''' (御幸通り, Imperial Road). The following year, in [[1911]] (Meiji 44), the Shin’enkai completed the {{ill|Jingu Chokokan|ja|神宮徴古館|12=draft|12=draft}}, which featured a Versailles-inspired front garden. The Chokokan included a state guest room used for resting by dignitaries such as [[Emperor Taisho]]. Further development in Kuratayama as an educational district followed the relocation of Jingu Kōgakkan (now {{ill|Kōgakkan University|ja|皇學館大学|12=draft|12=draft}}) to the area in [[1919]] (Taisho 8).
Due to the proximity of Onoue-no-Goryo, Yamatohime-no-Miya was constructed on a cleared section of Kuratayama. In [[1928]] (Showa 3), the tomb was officially designated as the '''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Burial Reference Site''' (倭姫命御陵墓参考地) and placed under the management of the [[Imperial Household Agency]]. == History == [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] is credited with establishing the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], creating rituals, and setting the foundation of [[Shinto]] practices. However, no shrine dedicated to her existed before the Meiji period.
Calls for a shrine arose around 1887 (Meiji 20), culminating in its official approval in 1912 (Taishō 1). The shrine was founded as a subsidiary of the Inner Shrine in 1923 (Taishō 12), following national approval in 1919 (Taishō 8).
During the 1945 (Shōwa 20) Ujiyamada air raids, the shukueiya was burned, but the main structure remained intact.
== deity == *Main deity: [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] (やまとひめのみこと)
== Grounds ==
Main Hall: Modeled after the Inner Shrine with [[Chigi]] and [[Katsuogi]], featuring a [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architectural style with thatched roofing and facing south.
Shukueiya: Provides blessings and talismans.
== Festivals == Festivals follow the Inner Shrine’s schedule, including:
[[Saitan-sai]] (January 1): Celebrating the new year and imperial lineage.
[[Kigensetsu]] (February 11): Commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s founding of the nation.
[[Kinen-sai]] (February 20): Praying for bountiful harvests.
[[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (October 24-25): Offering the year’s harvest to Amaterasu.
[[Niinamesai]] (November 26): Thanking the gods for the harvest.
== Worshipers == The Yamato-hime Shrine Reverence Association, founded in 1948 (Shōwa 23), holds regular events, including biannual festivals in May and November.
== Access == *Nearest station: [[Kintetsu]] [[Toba Line]], [[Isuzugawa Station]], 10 minutes on foot. *Parking: Available at the adjacent Jingu Chōkokan museum.
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also ==
* [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] * [[Ise Grand Shrine]] * [[List of 125 Shrines of Ise]] * [[Moto-Ise Shrines]]
== External Links ==
[https://www.isejingu.or.jp/about/outerbetsugu/ Yamatohime-no-miya] - Official Shrine Website
[http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m03/0313.html Yamatohime-no-miya - Jingu Association]

[[ja:倭姫宮]] [[sn:Yamatohime-no-Miya]] [[en:Yamatohime-no-Miya]] [[ja:やまとひめのみや]] [[sn:amatohime-no-Miya]]

{{Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamatohime-no-miya, shaden-2.jpg|256px|Yamatohime-no-miya]] | name=Yamatohime-no-miya | location=[[Mie Prefecture]], [[Ise City]], Kusube-cho, Akaidani 5[http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/shrine/area09.php "Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan | 125 Shrines 5"] (Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan, accessed May 3, 2015) | deity=[[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] | Rank=Betto Shrine of the [[Kōdaijingū|Inner Shrine]] | Foundation=[[1923]] (Taishō 12) | Main Structure=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates={{Coord|34|29|9.2|N|136|43|22.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Festival= }} '''Yamatohime-no-miya''' (倭姫宮) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Ise City]], Kusube-cho. It is a subsidiary shrine ("betsugu") of the [[Kōtai Jingu (Inner Shrine)|Inner Shrine]] of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]].
== Overview == [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_02.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii gate at the Omotesando entrance]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_03.JPG|thumb|200px|Chōzuya (purification fountain)]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_04.JPG|thumb|200px|Stone steps along the approach]]
In [[Ise Jingu]], there are 14 [[Betsugu]] (associated shrines) in addition to the [[Inner Shrine]] (Naiku) and the [[Outer Shrine]] (Geku). These include {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月讀宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|2=es|3=Tsukiyomi no miya (Naikū)|4=ja|5=月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tsukiyominomiya|ja|月夜見宮|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Izawa-no-Miya|ja|伊雑宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kazebi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and others. Among them, the creation date is clearly documented only for Yamatohime-no-Miya (倭姫宮), which was established on [[November 5, 1923]] (Taisho 12), making it the newest of the shrines.
The enshrined deity is {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the fourth imperial princess of the 11th emperor, [[Emperor Suinin]], as told in [[Japanese mythology]].
Like other outlying Betsugu shrines, the site has a Shukueiya (宿衛屋), a lodging for [[Shinto priest]]s who are stationed during visiting hours. They manage the distribution of talismans and amulets, as well as arrangements for [[kagura]] and ritual offerings.
=== location === Adjacent to Kuratayama (倉田山) lies "Ainoyama" (間の山), home to a small [[kofun]] (ancient tomb) called Onoue-no-Goryo (尾上御陵). This tomb is traditionally believed to be the burial site of Yamatohime-no-Mikoto. In honor of this connection, the temple town at the base of 常明寺 (Jōmyō-ji) was renamed '''Yamato Town''' (倭町, Yamatomachi) in [[1868]] (Meiji 1). There are conflicting theories about the tomb's shape, with some identifying it as a circular tomb ([[enzuka]]) and others as a keyhole-shaped mound ([[zenpō-kōen-fun]]).
In [[1886]] (Meiji 19), the residents of Ujiyamada Town formed the {{ill|Shin’enkai|ja|神苑会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a [[foundation]] focused on preventing fires from spreading to the shrine and preserving the shrine's aesthetic and cleanliness. In [[1891]] (Meiji 24), the Shin’enkai established the '''Jingu Agricultural Museum''' (神宮農業館), Japan’s oldest [[industrial museum]], near the Outer Shrine. In [[1905]] (Meiji 38), the museum was relocated to Kuratayama.
During [[Emperor Meiji]]'s [[Imperial visit]] to Ise Jingu, road improvements were necessary to accommodate [[automobiles]]. A road passing through Kuratayama was completed in [[1910]] (Meiji 43) and named '''Gokō-dōri''' (御幸通り, Imperial Road). The following year, in [[1911]] (Meiji 44), the Shin’enkai completed the {{ill|Jingu Chokokan|ja|神宮徴古館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which featured a Versailles-inspired front garden. The Chokokan included a state guest room used for resting by dignitaries such as [[Emperor Taisho]]. Further development in Kuratayama as an educational district followed the relocation of Jingu Kōgakkan (now {{ill|Kōgakkan University|ja|皇學館大学|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) to the area in [[1919]] (Taisho 8).
Due to the proximity of Onoue-no-Goryo, Yamatohime-no-Miya was constructed on a cleared section of Kuratayama. In [[1928]] (Showa 3), the tomb was officially designated as the '''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Burial Reference Site''' (倭姫命御陵墓参考地) and placed under the management of the [[Imperial Household Agency]]. == History == [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] is credited with establishing the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], creating rituals, and setting the foundation of [[Shinto]] practices. However, no shrine dedicated to her existed before the Meiji period.
Calls for a shrine arose around 1887 (Meiji 20), culminating in its official approval in 1912 (Taishō 1). The shrine was founded as a subsidiary of the Inner Shrine in 1923 (Taishō 12), following national approval in 1919 (Taishō 8).
During the 1945 (Shōwa 20) Ujiyamada air raids, the shukueiya was burned, but the main structure remained intact.
== deity == *Main deity: [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] (やまとひめのみこと)
== Grounds ==
Main Hall: Modeled after the Inner Shrine with [[Chigi]] and [[Katsuogi]], featuring a [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architectural style with thatched roofing and facing south.
Shukueiya: Provides blessings and talismans.
== Festivals == Festivals follow the Inner Shrine’s schedule, including:
[[Saitan-sai]] (January 1): Celebrating the new year and imperial lineage.
[[Kigensetsu]] (February 11): Commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s founding of the nation.
[[Kinen-sai]] (February 20): Praying for bountiful harvests.
[[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (October 24-25): Offering the year’s harvest to Amaterasu.
[[Niinamesai]] (November 26): Thanking the gods for the harvest.
== Worshipers == The Yamato-hime Shrine Reverence Association, founded in 1948 (Shōwa 23), holds regular events, including biannual festivals in May and November.
== Access == *Nearest station: [[Kintetsu]] [[Toba Line]], [[Isuzugawa Station]], 10 minutes on foot. *Parking: Available at the adjacent Jingu Chōkokan museum.
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also ==
* [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] * [[Ise Grand Shrine]] * [[List of 125 Shrines of Ise]] * [[Moto-Ise Shrines]]
== External Links ==

[http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m03/0313.html Yamatohime-no-miya - Jingu Association]

revision.comment: 40 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:倭姫宮 up to rev 95525833
revision.text:
{{神社 | 画像=[[File:Yamatohime-no-miya, shaden-2.jpg|256px|倭姫宮]] | 名称=倭姫宮 | 所在地=[[三重県]][[伊勢市]]楠部町字赤井谷5<ref>[http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/shrine/area09.php 『式年遷宮記念 せんぐう館｜125社5』]（式年遷宮記念 せんぐう館、2015年5月3日閲覧）</ref> | 祭神=[[倭姫命]] | 社格=皇大神宮別宮 | 創建=[[1923年]]（[[大正]]12年） | 本殿=[[神明造]] | 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|29|9.2|N|136|43|22.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | 例祭= }} '''倭姫宮'''（やまとひめのみや）は、[[三重県]][[伊勢市]]楠部町にある[[神社]]。[[皇大神宮|内宮（皇大神宮）]]の別宮である。
== 概要 == [[Image:Yamatohime-no-miya_02.JPG|thumb|200px|表参道口の鳥居]] [[Image:Yamatohime-no-miya_03.JPG|thumb|200px|手水舎]] [[Image:Yamatohime-no-miya_04.JPG|thumb|200px|参道の石段]]
[[伊勢神宮]]において、内宮と[[豊受大神宮|外宮]]と合わせ別宮は[[荒祭宮]]、[[多賀宮]]、[[月讀宮]]、[[土宮]]、[[月夜見宮]]、[[瀧原宮]]、[[伊雑宮]]、[[風日祈宮]]、[[風宮]]など14宮あるが、そのうち創建が明確であるのは倭姫宮1宮のみで、[[1923年]]（[[大正]]12年）[[11月5日]]ともっとも新しい。
祭神は、[[日本神話]]で第11代[[垂仁天皇]]の第四皇女と伝えられる[[倭姫命]]である。
ほかの境外別宮と同様に、[[神職]]が参拝時間内に常駐する宿衛屋（しゅくえいや）があり、お札・お守りの授与や、[[神楽]]や御饌の取次ぎを行なっている。
=== 所在地 === 倉田山に隣接する'''間の山'''（あいのやま）に尾上御陵（おべごりょう）と呼ばれる小さな[[古墳]]がある。この尾上御陵を倭姫命の陵墓とする伝承にちなみ、御陵のある常明寺門前町は[[1868年]]（[[明治]]元年）に'''倭町'''（やまとまち）と改称した。尾上御陵は[[円墳]]とする説と[[前方後円墳]]とする説がある。
宇治山田町民を中心に[[1886年]]（明治19年）に、民家から発生した火災の神宮への延焼を防止するなどを目的とする[[財団法人]][[神苑会]]が結成され、神苑の美観と清浄を確保するなどの活動を行なっていた。神苑会は[[1891年]]（明治24年）に外宮前に日本最古の[[博物館|産業博物館]]である'''神宮農業館'''を創設した。神宮農業館は[[1905年]]（明治38年）に倉田山に移築された。
[[明治天皇]]の[[行幸]]で神宮へ参拝するための道路の整備が問題とされ、[[自動車]]が通行できる道路が倉田山を経由して作られることになった。この道路は[[1910年]]（明治43年）に竣工し、'''御幸通り'''と名付けられた。[[1911年]]（明治44年）には神苑会による、[[ヴェルサイユ宮殿]]を模した前庭を持つ[[神宮徴古館]]が完成した。神宮徴古館には貴賓室が設けられ、[[大正天皇]]などの休息に使用された。[[1882年]]（明治15年）に内宮近くに創立された神宮皇學館（現在の[[皇學館大学]]）が[[1919年]]（[[大正]]8年）に移転するなど、文教地区としての倉田山開発が進んだ。
付近に尾上御陵があることもあり、倭姫宮は倉田山の一角を開削して作られることになった。尾上御陵は倭姫宮創建後の[[1928年]]（[[昭和]]3年）10月に'''倭姫命御陵墓参考地'''に指定され、[[宮内庁]]に管理されている。
== 歴史 == [[倭姫命]]は[[天照大神]]を祀る宮を定めるため、数国を経たのち現在地に[[伊勢神宮]]を創建し、[[祭祀]]や[[神職]]の制度を定め、[[神道]]の基礎を作ったと伝えられるが、[[明治]]以前は倭姫命を祀る神社は作られなかった。
[[1887年]]（明治20年）頃より'''宇治山田町'''（現在の[[伊勢市]]中心部）の住民を中心に、倭姫命を祀る神社を創立すべきという声が高まった。宇治山田町は[[1906年]]（明治39年）に市制を施行し'''宇治山田市'''となり、市長が先頭にたち倭姫命を祀る神社の創立の許可を[[1912年]]（大正元年）に国会に請願し、[[1919年]]（大正8年）に[[帝国議会]]で創立の予算が可決され、[[1921年]]（大正10年）1月4日には内宮別宮としての創立が決定し、[[1923年]]（大正12年）11月5日、外宮と内宮の中間に近い'''倉田山'''にて鎮座祭が執り行われ創立された。
[[1945年]]（昭和20年）の[[宇治山田空襲]]では、[[2月15日]]に付近で[[焼夷弾]]による火災が発生<ref>三重県歴史教育者協議会 編（2006）：299ページ</ref>、[[7月29日]]に宿衛屋が焼失したが、本殿は無事であった<ref>矢野（2006）：185ページ</ref>。
== 祭神 == *主祭神 - 倭姫命（やまとひめのみこと）
== 境内 == *本殿 - 内宮に準じ、[[千木・鰹木|内削ぎの千木と、偶数の6本の鰹木]]を持つ[[神明造]]で萱葺で、南面する。周囲には門のある瑞垣と玉垣が配される。 *宿衛屋
== 祭事 == [[皇大神宮]]に準じた祭事が行なわれ、[[祈年祭]]、月次祭、[[神嘗祭]]、[[新嘗祭]]の諸祭には皇室からの幣帛（へいはく）がある。
* 1月 ** [[歳旦祭]]（さいたんさい）（1月1日） 新年を祝い、皇位の無窮を祈る祭り。 ** [[元始祭]]（げんしさい）（1月3日） 年始にあたり、天津日嗣（あまつひつぎ 皇統）の本始を祝う祭り。 * 2月 ** [[建国記念祭]]（けんこくきねんさい）（2月11日） [[神武天皇]]の建国の創祀を祝う祭り。昭和23年に廃止された紀元節祭（きげんせつさい）を改名し、1967年（昭和42年）に復興した。 ** [[祈年祭]]（きねんさい）（2月20日） 年の初めに穀物の豊穣と国家の安泰を祈る祭り。 * 5月 ** 風日祈祭（かざひのみさい）（5月14日） 外宮内宮のほか、別宮末社摂社などに幣帛を供進し、風雨の災いなく五穀豊穣であるように祈る神事。 * 6月 ** 月次祭（つきなみさい）（6月24-25日） 6月と12月の11日に国家の平安と天皇の福寿を祈った祭り。伊勢神宮では神嘗祭と合わせて三節祭または三時祭と呼ぶ。 * 8月 ** 風日祈祭（かざひのみさい）（8月4日） * 10月 ** [[神嘗祭]]（かんなめさい）（10月24-25日） 神官がその年に収穫された穀物を「[[神饌|大御饌（おおみけ）]]」として天照大神に奉献する祭り。 * 11月 ** [[新嘗祭]]（にいなめさい）（11月26日） 天皇がその年に収穫された穀物を神に供えるとともに自らも食し、収穫を感謝する祭り。 * 12月 ** [[天長祭]]（てんちょうさい）（12月23日） [[明仁天皇]]の誕生日を祝う祭り。 ** 月次祭（つきなみさい）（12月24-25日）
== 信者団体 == [[1948年]]（昭和23年）に伊勢市在住の崇敬者を中心に御杖代講（みつえしろこう）が発足され、のちに倭姫宮御杖代講奉賛会と改称した。倭姫宮御杖代講奉賛会は毎年5月と11月の5日に例大祭を行なうなどの活動をしている。
== 交通 == 三重県道37号鳥羽松阪線沿いの三重交通徴古館前バス停付近の表参道口がある。
* 最寄駅：[[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[近鉄鳥羽線|鳥羽線]][[五十鈴川駅]]から北へ徒歩約10分 ** [[東海旅客鉄道|JR東海]][[参宮線]][[伊勢市駅]]から約3km * 最寄バス停：[[三重交通]]徴古館前バス停 * 一般国道：[[三重県道37号鳥羽松阪線]]（[[御幸道路]]）沿い * 最寄インターチェンジ：[[伊勢自動車道]][[伊勢インターチェンジ]]から約1km * 駐車場：隣接する神宮徴古館に駐車場がある。駐車場からは徴古館建物前の裏参道口からの方が便利である。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考資料 == * 三重県歴史教育者協議会 編『三重の戦争遺跡 増補改訂版』[[つむぎ出版]]、2006年8月15日、314p. ISBN 4-87668-151-1 * 矢野憲一『伊勢神宮―知られざる杜のうち』角川選書402、[[角川学芸出版]]、平成18年11月10日、270p. ISBN 4-04-703402-9 * 『伊勢神宮』（[[桜井勝之進]]著、学生社発行） * 『倭姫宮参拝のしおり』（発行：神宮司庁）
== 関連項目 == * [[倭姫命]] * [[伊勢神宮]] * [[神宮125社の一覧]] * [[元伊勢]]
== 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Yamatohime-no-miya (Naiku)}} * [https://www.isejingu.or.jp/about/outerbetsugu/ 倭姫宮] - 神宮司庁 * [http://www.yamatohime.jp/index.html 倭姫宮御杖代奉賛会] ** [http://www.yamatohime.jp/html/page_a.html 倭姫宮] * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m03/0313.html 倭姫宮（やまとひめのみや） - 神宮会館] - 一般財団法人伊勢神宮崇敬会 * {{PDFlink|[https://www.isejingu.or.jp/download/pdf/pamphlet_yamatohime.pdf 倭姫宮 - 伊勢神宮]}} - 神宮司庁

== 概要 == [[Image:Yamatohime-no-miya_02.JPG|thumb|200px|表参道口の鳥居]] [[Image:Yamatohime-no-miya_03.JPG|thumb|200px|手水舎]] [[Image:Yamatohime-no-miya_04.JPG|thumb|200px|参道の石段]]
[[伊勢神宮]]において、内宮と[[豊受大神宮|外宮]]と合わせ別宮は[[荒祭宮]]、[[多賀宮]]、[[月讀宮]]、[[土宮]]、[[月夜見宮]]、[[瀧原宮]]、[[伊雑宮]]、[[風日祈宮]]、[[風宮]]など14宮あるが、そのうち創建が明確であるのは倭姫宮1宮のみで、[[1923年]]（[[大正]]12年）[[11月5日]]ともっとも新しい。
祭神は、[[日本神話]]で第11代[[垂仁天皇]]の第四皇女と伝えられる[[倭姫命]]である。
ほかの境外別宮と同様に、[[神職]]が参拝時間内に常駐する宿衛屋（しゅくえいや）があり、お札・お守りの授与や、[[神楽]]や御饌の取次ぎを行なっている。
=== 所在地 === 倉田山に隣接する'''間の山'''（あいのやま）に尾上御陵（おべごりょう）と呼ばれる小さな[[古墳]]がある。この尾上御陵を倭姫命の陵墓とする伝承にちなみ、御陵のある常明寺門前町は[[1868年]]（[[明治]]元年）に'''倭町'''（やまとまち）と改称した。尾上御陵は[[円墳]]とする説と[[前方後円墳]]とする説がある。
宇治山田町民を中心に[[1886年]]（明治19年）に、民家から発生した火災の神宮への延焼を防止するなどを目的とする[[財団法人]][[神苑会]]が結成され、神苑の美観と清浄を確保するなどの活動を行なっていた。神苑会は[[1891年]]（明治24年）に外宮前に日本最古の[[博物館|産業博物館]]である'''神宮農業館'''を創設した。神宮農業館は[[1905年]]（明治38年）に倉田山に移築された。
[[明治天皇]]の[[行幸]]で神宮へ参拝するための道路の整備が問題とされ、[[自動車]]が通行できる道路が倉田山を経由して作られることになった。この道路は[[1910年]]（明治43年）に竣工し、'''御幸通り'''と名付けられた。[[1911年]]（明治44年）には神苑会による、[[ヴェルサイユ宮殿]]を模した前庭を持つ[[神宮徴古館]]が完成した。神宮徴古館には貴賓室が設けられ、[[大正天皇]]などの休息に使用された。[[1882年]]（明治15年）に内宮近くに創立された神宮皇學館（現在の[[皇學館大学]]）が[[1919年]]（[[大正]]8年）に移転するなど、文教地区としての倉田山開発が進んだ。
付近に尾上御陵があることもあり、倭姫宮は倉田山の一角を開削して作られることになった。尾上御陵は倭姫宮創建後の[[1928年]]（[[昭和]]3年）10月に'''倭姫命御陵墓参考地'''に指定され、[[宮内庁]]に管理されている。
== 歴史 == [[倭姫命]]は[[天照大神]]を祀る宮を定めるため、数国を経たのち現在地に[[伊勢神宮]]を創建し、[[祭祀]]や[[神職]]の制度を定め、[[神道]]の基礎を作ったと伝えられるが、[[明治]]以前は倭姫命を祀る神社は作られなかった。
[[1887年]]（明治20年）頃より'''宇治山田町'''（現在の[[伊勢市]]中心部）の住民を中心に、倭姫命を祀る神社を創立すべきという声が高まった。宇治山田町は[[1906年]]（明治39年）に市制を施行し'''宇治山田市'''となり、市長が先頭にたち倭姫命を祀る神社の創立の許可を[[1912年]]（大正元年）に国会に請願し、[[1919年]]（大正8年）に[[帝国議会]]で創立の予算が可決され、[[1921年]]（大正10年）1月4日には内宮別宮としての創立が決定し、[[1923年]]（大正12年）11月5日、外宮と内宮の中間に近い'''倉田山'''にて鎮座祭が執り行われ創立された。
[[1945年]]（昭和20年）の[[宇治山田空襲]]では、[[2月15日]]に付近で[[焼夷弾]]による火災が発生<ref>三重県歴史教育者協議会 編（2006）：299ページ</ref>、[[7月29日]]に宿衛屋が焼失したが、本殿は無事であった<ref>矢野（2006）：185ページ</ref>。
== 祭神 == *主祭神 - 倭姫命（やまとひめのみこと）
== 境内 == *本殿 - 内宮に準じ、[[千木・鰹木|内削ぎの千木と、偶数の6本の鰹木]]を持つ[[神明造]]で萱葺で、南面する。周囲には門のある瑞垣と玉垣が配される。 *宿衛屋
== 祭事 == [[皇大神宮]]に準じた祭事が行なわれ、[[祈年祭]]、月次祭、[[神嘗祭]]、[[新嘗祭]]の諸祭には皇室からの幣帛（へいはく）がある。
* 1月 ** [[歳旦祭]]（さいたんさい）（1月1日） 新年を祝い、皇位の無窮を祈る祭り。 ** [[元始祭]]（げんしさい）（1月3日） 年始にあたり、天津日嗣（あまつひつぎ 皇統）の本始を祝う祭り。 * 2月 ** [[建国記念祭]]（けんこくきねんさい）（2月11日） [[神武天皇]]の建国の創祀を祝う祭り。昭和23年に廃止された紀元節祭（きげんせつさい）を改名し、1967年（昭和42年）に復興した。 ** [[祈年祭]]（きねんさい）（2月20日） 年の初めに穀物の豊穣と国家の安泰を祈る祭り。 * 5月 ** 風日祈祭（かざひのみさい）（5月14日） 外宮内宮のほか、別宮末社摂社などに幣帛を供進し、風雨の災いなく五穀豊穣であるように祈る神事。 * 6月 ** 月次祭（つきなみさい）（6月24-25日） 6月と12月の11日に国家の平安と天皇の福寿を祈った祭り。伊勢神宮では神嘗祭と合わせて三節祭または三時祭と呼ぶ。 * 8月 ** 風日祈祭（かざひのみさい）（8月4日） * 10月 ** [[神嘗祭]]（かんなめさい）（10月24-25日） 神官がその年に収穫された穀物を「[[神饌|大御饌（おおみけ）]]」として天照大神に奉献する祭り。 * 11月 ** [[新嘗祭]]（にいなめさい）（11月26日） 天皇がその年に収穫された穀物を神に供えるとともに自らも食し、収穫を感謝する祭り。 * 12月 ** [[天長祭]]（てんちょうさい）（12月23日） [[明仁天皇]]の誕生日を祝う祭り。 ** 月次祭（つきなみさい）（12月24-25日）
== 信者団体 == [[1948年]]（昭和23年）に伊勢市在住の崇敬者を中心に御杖代講（みつえしろこう）が発足され、のちに倭姫宮御杖代講奉賛会と改称した。倭姫宮御杖代講奉賛会は毎年5月と11月の5日に例大祭を行なうなどの活動をしている。
== 交通 == 三重県道37号鳥羽松阪線沿いの三重交通徴古館前バス停付近の表参道口がある。
* 最寄駅：[[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[近鉄鳥羽線|鳥羽線]][[五十鈴川駅]]から北へ徒歩約10分 ** [[東海旅客鉄道|JR東海]][[参宮線]][[伊勢市駅]]から約3km * 最寄バス停：[[三重交通]]徴古館前バス停 * 一般国道：[[三重県道37号鳥羽松阪線]]（[[御幸道路]]）沿い * 最寄インターチェンジ：[[伊勢自動車道]][[伊勢インターチェンジ]]から約1km * 駐車場：隣接する神宮徴古館に駐車場がある。駐車場からは徴古館建物前の裏参道口からの方が便利である。

{{デフォルトソート:やまとひめのみや}} [[Category:伊勢市の神社]] [[Category:神宮125社]] [[Category:倉田山]] [[Category:1923年開業の施設]] [[Category:2014年竣工の日本の建築物]]

{{Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamatohime-no-miya, shaden-2.jpg|256px|Yamatohime-no-miya]] | name=Yamatohime-no-miya | location=[[Mie Prefecture]], [[Ise City]], Kusube-cho, Akaidani 5[http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/shrine/area09.php "Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan | 125 Shrines 5"] (Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan, accessed May 3, 2015) | deity=[[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] | Rank=Betto Shrine of the [[Kōdaijingū|Inner Shrine]] | Foundation=[[1923]] (Taishō 12) | Main Structure=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates={{Coord|34|29|9.2|N|136|43|22.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Festival= }} '''Yamatohime-no-miya''' (倭姫宮) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Ise City]], Kusube-cho. It is a subsidiary shrine ("betsugu") of the [[Kōtai Jingu (Inner Shrine)|Inner Shrine]] of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]].
== Overview == [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_02.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii gate at the Omotesando entrance]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_03.JPG|thumb|200px|Chōzuya (purification fountain)]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_04.JPG|thumb|200px|Stone steps along the approach]]
In [[Ise Jingu]], there are 14 [[Betsugu]] (associated shrines) in addition to the [[Inner Shrine]] (Naiku) and the [[Outer Shrine]] (Geku). These include {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月讀宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|2=es|3=Tsukiyomi no miya (Naikū)|4=ja|5=月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tsukiyominomiya|ja|月夜見宮|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Izawa-no-Miya|ja|伊雑宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kazebi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and others. Among them, the creation date is clearly documented only for Yamatohime-no-Miya (倭姫宮), which was established on [[November 5, 1923]] (Taisho 12), making it the newest of the shrines.
The enshrined deity is {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the fourth imperial princess of the 11th emperor, [[Emperor Suinin]], as told in [[Japanese mythology]].
Like other outlying Betsugu shrines, the site has a Shukueiya (宿衛屋), a lodging for [[Shinto priest]]s who are stationed during visiting hours. They manage the distribution of talismans and amulets, as well as arrangements for [[kagura]] and ritual offerings.
=== location === Adjacent to Kuratayama (倉田山) lies "Ainoyama" (間の山), home to a small [[kofun]] (ancient tomb) called Onoue-no-Goryo (尾上御陵). This tomb is traditionally believed to be the burial site of Yamatohime-no-Mikoto. In honor of this connection, the temple town at the base of 常明寺 (Jōmyō-ji) was renamed '''Yamato Town''' (倭町, Yamatomachi) in [[1868]] (Meiji 1). There are conflicting theories about the tomb's shape, with some identifying it as a circular tomb ([[enzuka]]) and others as a keyhole-shaped mound ([[zenpō-kōen-fun]]).
In [[1886]] (Meiji 19), the residents of Ujiyamada Town formed the {{ill|Shin’enkai|ja|神苑会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a [[foundation]] focused on preventing fires from spreading to the shrine and preserving the shrine's aesthetic and cleanliness. In [[1891]] (Meiji 24), the Shin’enkai established the '''Jingu Agricultural Museum''' (神宮農業館), Japan’s oldest [[industrial museum]], near the Outer Shrine. In [[1905]] (Meiji 38), the museum was relocated to Kuratayama.
During [[Emperor Meiji]]'s [[Imperial visit]] to Ise Jingu, road improvements were necessary to accommodate [[automobiles]]. A road passing through Kuratayama was completed in [[1910]] (Meiji 43) and named '''Gokō-dōri''' (御幸通り, Imperial Road). The following year, in [[1911]] (Meiji 44), the Shin’enkai completed the {{ill|Jingu Chokokan|ja|神宮徴古館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which featured a Versailles-inspired front garden. The Chokokan included a state guest room used for resting by dignitaries such as [[Emperor Taisho]]. Further development in Kuratayama as an educational district followed the relocation of Jingu Kōgakkan (now {{ill|Kōgakkan University|ja|皇學館大学|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) to the area in [[1919]] (Taisho 8).
Due to the proximity of Onoue-no-Goryo, Yamatohime-no-Miya was constructed on a cleared section of Kuratayama. In [[1928]] (Showa 3), the tomb was officially designated as the '''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Burial Reference Site''' (倭姫命御陵墓参考地) and placed under the management of the [[Imperial Household Agency]]. == History == [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] is credited with establishing the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], creating rituals, and setting the foundation of [[Shinto]] practices. However, no shrine dedicated to her existed before the Meiji period.
Calls for a shrine arose around 1887 (Meiji 20), culminating in its official approval in 1912 (Taishō 1). The shrine was founded as a subsidiary of the Inner Shrine in 1923 (Taishō 12), following national approval in 1919 (Taishō 8).
During the 1945 (Shōwa 20) Ujiyamada air raids, the shukueiya was burned, but the main structure remained intact.
== deity == *Main deity: [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] (やまとひめのみこと)
== Grounds ==
Main Hall: Modeled after the Inner Shrine with [[Chigi]] and [[Katsuogi]], featuring a [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architectural style with thatched roofing and facing south.
Shukueiya: Provides blessings and talismans.
== Festivals == Festivals follow the Inner Shrine’s schedule, including:
[[Saitan-sai]] (January 1): Celebrating the new year and imperial lineage.
[[Kigensetsu]] (February 11): Commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s founding of the nation.
[[Kinen-sai]] (February 20): Praying for bountiful harvests.
[[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (October 24-25): Offering the year’s harvest to Amaterasu.
[[Niinamesai]] (November 26): Thanking the gods for the harvest.
== Worshipers == The Yamato-hime Shrine Reverence Association, founded in 1948 (Shōwa 23), holds regular events, including biannual festivals in May and November.
== Access == *Nearest station: [[Kintetsu]] [[Toba Line]], [[Isuzugawa Station]], 10 minutes on foot. *Parking: Available at the adjacent Jingu Chōkokan museum.

Shukueiya: Provides blessings and talismans.
== Festivals == Festivals follow the Inner Shrine’s schedule, including:
[[Saitan-sai]] (January 1): Celebrating the new year and imperial lineage.
[[Kigensetsu]] (February 11): Commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s founding of the nation.
[[Kinen-sai]] (February 20): Praying for bountiful harvests.
[[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (October 24-25): Offering the year’s harvest to Amaterasu.
[[Niinamesai]] (November 26): Thanking the gods for the harvest.
== Worshipers == The Yamato-hime Shrine Reverence Association, founded in 1948 (Shōwa 23), holds regular events, including biannual festivals in May and November.
== Access == *Nearest station: [[Kintetsu]] [[Toba Line]], [[Isuzugawa Station]], 10 minutes on foot. *Parking: Available at the adjacent Jingu Chōkokan museum.

[[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_02.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii gate at the Omotesando entrance]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_03.JPG|thumb|200px|Chōzuya (purification fountain)]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_04.JPG|thumb|200px|Stone steps along the approach]] [[ja:倭姫宮]] [[sn:Yamatohime-no-Miya]] [[en:Yamatohime-no-Miya]] [[ja:やまとひめのみや]] [[sn:amatohime-no-Miya]]

{{Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamatohime-no-miya, shaden-2.jpg|256px|Yamatohime-no-miya]] | name=Yamatohime-no-miya | location=[[Mie Prefecture]], [[Ise City]], Kusube-cho, Akaidani 5[http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/shrine/area09.php "Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan | 125 Shrines 5"] (Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan, accessed May 3, 2015) | deity=[[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] | Rank=Betto Shrine of the [[Kōdaijingū|Inner Shrine]] | Foundation=[[1923]] (Taishō 12) | Main Structure=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates={{Coord|34|29|9.2|N|136|43|22.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Festival= }} '''Yamatohime-no-miya''' (倭姫宮) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Ise City]], Kusube-cho. It is a subsidiary shrine ("betsugu") of the [[Kōtai Jingu (Inner Shrine)|Inner Shrine]] of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]].
== Overview ==
In [[Ise Jingu]], there are 14 [[Betsugu]] (associated shrines) in addition to the [[Inner Shrine]] (Naiku) and the [[Outer Shrine]] (Geku). These include {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月讀宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|2=es|3=Tsukiyomi no miya (Naikū)|4=ja|5=月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tsukiyominomiya|ja|月夜見宮|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Izawa-no-Miya|ja|伊雑宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kazebi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and others. Among them, the creation date is clearly documented only for Yamatohime-no-Miya (倭姫宮), which was established on [[November 5, 1923]] (Taisho 12), making it the newest of the shrines.
The enshrined deity is {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the fourth imperial princess of the 11th emperor, [[Emperor Suinin]], as told in [[Japanese mythology]].
Like other outlying Betsugu shrines, the site has a Shukueiya (宿衛屋), a lodging for [[Shinto priest]]s who are stationed during visiting hours. They manage the distribution of talismans and amulets, as well as arrangements for [[kagura]] and ritual offerings.
=== location === Adjacent to Kuratayama (倉田山) lies "Ainoyama" (間の山), home to a small [[kofun]] (ancient tomb) called Onoue-no-Goryo (尾上御陵). This tomb is traditionally believed to be the burial site of Yamatohime-no-Mikoto. In honor of this connection, the temple town at the base of 常明寺 (Jōmyō-ji) was renamed '''Yamato Town''' (倭町, Yamatomachi) in [[1868]] (Meiji 1). There are conflicting theories about the tomb's shape, with some identifying it as a circular tomb ([[enzuka]]) and others as a keyhole-shaped mound ([[zenpō-kōen-fun]]).
In [[1886]] (Meiji 19), the residents of Ujiyamada Town formed the {{ill|Shin’enkai|ja|神苑会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a [[foundation]] focused on preventing fires from spreading to the shrine and preserving the shrine's aesthetic and cleanliness. In [[1891]] (Meiji 24), the Shin’enkai established the '''Jingu Agricultural Museum''' (神宮農業館), Japan’s oldest [[industrial museum]], near the Outer Shrine. In [[1905]] (Meiji 38), the museum was relocated to Kuratayama.
During [[Emperor Meiji]]'s [[Imperial visit]] to Ise Jingu, road improvements were necessary to accommodate [[automobiles]]. A road passing through Kuratayama was completed in [[1910]] (Meiji 43) and named '''Gokō-dōri''' (御幸通り, Imperial Road). The following year, in [[1911]] (Meiji 44), the Shin’enkai completed the {{ill|Jingu Chokokan|ja|神宮徴古館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which featured a Versailles-inspired front garden. The Chokokan included a state guest room used for resting by dignitaries such as [[Emperor Taisho]]. Further development in Kuratayama as an educational district followed the relocation of Jingu Kōgakkan (now {{ill|Kōgakkan University|ja|皇學館大学|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) to the area in [[1919]] (Taisho 8).
Due to the proximity of Onoue-no-Goryo, Yamatohime-no-Miya was constructed on a cleared section of Kuratayama. In [[1928]] (Showa 3), the tomb was officially designated as the '''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Burial Reference Site''' (倭姫命御陵墓参考地) and placed under the management of the [[Imperial Household Agency]]. == History == [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] is credited with establishing the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], creating rituals, and setting the foundation of [[Shinto]] practices. However, no shrine dedicated to her existed before the Meiji period.
Calls for a shrine arose around 1887 (Meiji 20), culminating in its official approval in 1912 (Taishō 1). The shrine was founded as a subsidiary of the Inner Shrine in 1923 (Taishō 12), following national approval in 1919 (Taishō 8).
During the 1945 (Shōwa 20) Ujiyamada air raids, the shukueiya was burned, but the main structure remained intact.
== deity == *Main deity: [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] (やまとひめのみこと)

[[Saitan-sai]] (January 1): Celebrating the new year and imperial lineage.
[[Kigensetsu]] (February 11): Commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s founding of the nation.
[[Kinen-sai]] (February 20): Praying for bountiful harvests.
[[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (October 24-25): Offering the year’s harvest to Amaterasu.
[[Niinamesai]] (November 26): Thanking the gods for the harvest.
== Worshipers == The Yamato-hime Shrine Reverence Association, founded in 1948 (Shōwa 23), holds regular events, including biannual festivals in May and November.
== Access == *Nearest station: [[Kintetsu]] [[Toba Line]], [[Isuzugawa Station]], 10 minutes on foot. *Parking: Available at the adjacent Jingu Chōkokan museum.

[[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_02.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii gate at the Omotesando entrance]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_03.JPG|thumb|200px|Chōzuya (purification fountain)]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_04.JPG|thumb|200px|Stone steps along the approach]] [[ja:倭姫宮]] [[sn:Yamatohime-no-Miya]] [[en:Yamatohime-no-Miya]] [[ja:やまとひめのみや]] [[sn:amatohime-no-Miya]]

{{Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamatohime-no-miya, shaden-2.jpg|256px|Yamatohime-no-miya]] | name=Yamatohime-no-miya | location=[[Mie Prefecture]], [[Ise City]], Kusube-cho, Akaidani 5[http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/shrine/area09.php "Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan | 125 Shrines 5"] (Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan, accessed May 3, 2015) | deity=[[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] | Rank=Betto Shrine of the [[Kōdaijingū|Inner Shrine]] | Foundation=[[1923]] (Taishō 12) | Main Structure=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates={{Coord|34|29|9.2|N|136|43|22.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Festival= }} '''Yamatohime-no-miya''' (倭姫宮) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Ise City]], Kusube-cho. It is a subsidiary shrine ("betsugu") of the [[Kōtai Jingu (Inner Shrine)|Inner Shrine]] of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]].
== Overview ==
In [[Ise Jingu]], there are 14 [[Betsugu]] (associated shrines) in addition to the [[Inner Shrine]] (Naiku) and the [[Outer Shrine]] (Geku). These include {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月讀宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|2=es|3=Tsukiyomi no miya (Naikū)|4=ja|5=月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Tsukiyominomiya|ja|月夜見宮|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Izawa-no-Miya|ja|伊雑宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kazebi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and others. Among them, the creation date is clearly documented only for Yamatohime-no-Miya (倭姫宮), which was established on [[November 5, 1923]] (Taisho 12), making it the newest of the shrines.
The enshrined deity is {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the fourth imperial princess of the 11th emperor, [[Emperor Suinin]], as told in [[Japanese mythology]].
Like other outlying Betsugu shrines, the site has a Shukueiya (宿衛屋), a lodging for [[Shinto priest]]s who are stationed during visiting hours. They manage the distribution of talismans and amulets, as well as arrangements for [[kagura]] and ritual offerings.
=== location === Adjacent to Kuratayama (倉田山) lies "Ainoyama" (間の山), home to a small [[kofun]] (ancient tomb) called Onoue-no-Goryo (尾上御陵). This tomb is traditionally believed to be the burial site of Yamatohime-no-Mikoto. In honor of this connection, the temple town at the base of 常明寺 (Jōmyō-ji) was renamed '''Yamato Town''' (倭町, Yamatomachi) in [[1868]] (Meiji 1). There are conflicting theories about the tomb's shape, with some identifying it as a circular tomb ([[enzuka]]) and others as a keyhole-shaped mound ([[zenpō-kōen-fun]]).
In [[1886]] (Meiji 19), the residents of Ujiyamada Town formed the {{ill|Shin’enkai|ja|神苑会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a [[foundation]] focused on preventing fires from spreading to the shrine and preserving the shrine's aesthetic and cleanliness. In [[1891]] (Meiji 24), the Shin’enkai established the '''Jingu Agricultural Museum''' (神宮農業館), Japan’s oldest [[industrial museum]], near the Outer Shrine. In [[1905]] (Meiji 38), the museum was relocated to Kuratayama.
During [[Emperor Meiji]]'s [[Imperial visit]] to Ise Jingu, road improvements were necessary to accommodate [[automobiles]]. A road passing through Kuratayama was completed in [[1910]] (Meiji 43) and named '''Gokō-dōri''' (御幸通り, Imperial Road). The following year, in [[1911]] (Meiji 44), the Shin’enkai completed the {{ill|Jingu Chokokan|ja|神宮徴古館|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which featured a Versailles-inspired front garden. The Chokokan included a state guest room used for resting by dignitaries such as [[Emperor Taisho]]. Further development in Kuratayama as an educational district followed the relocation of Jingu Kōgakkan (now {{ill|Kōgakkan University|ja|皇學館大学|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) to the area in [[1919]] (Taisho 8).
Due to the proximity of Onoue-no-Goryo, Yamatohime-no-Miya was constructed on a cleared section of Kuratayama. In [[1928]] (Showa 3), the tomb was officially designated as the '''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Burial Reference Site''' (倭姫命御陵墓参考地) and placed under the management of the [[Imperial Household Agency]]. == History == [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] is credited with establishing the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], creating rituals, and setting the foundation of [[Shinto]] practices. However, no shrine dedicated to her existed before the Meiji period.
Calls for a shrine arose around 1887 (Meiji 20), culminating in its official approval in 1912 (Taishō 1). The shrine was founded as a subsidiary of the Inner Shrine in 1923 (Taishō 12), following national approval in 1919 (Taishō 8).
During the 1945 (Shōwa 20) Ujiyamada air raids, the shukueiya was burned, but the main structure remained intact.
== deity == *Main deity: [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] (やまとひめのみこと)
== Grounds ==
Main Hall: Modeled after the Inner Shrine with [[Chigi]] and [[Katsuogi]], featuring a [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architectural style with thatched roofing and facing south.
Shukueiya: Provides blessings and talismans.
== Festivals == Festivals follow the Inner Shrine’s schedule, including:
[[Saitan-sai]] (January 1): Celebrating the new year and imperial lineage.
[[Kigensetsu]] (February 11): Commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s founding of the nation.
[[Kinen-sai]] (February 20): Praying for bountiful harvests.
[[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (October 24-25): Offering the year’s harvest to Amaterasu.
[[Niinamesai]] (November 26): Thanking the gods for the harvest.
== Worshipers == The Yamato-hime Shrine Reverence Association, founded in 1948 (Shōwa 23), holds regular events, including biannual festivals in May and November.
== Access == *Nearest station: [[Kintetsu]] [[Toba Line]], [[Isuzugawa Station]], 10 minutes on foot. *Parking: Available at the adjacent Jingu Chōkokan museum.
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also ==

Calls for a shrine arose around 1887 (Meiji 20), culminating in its official approval in 1912 (Taishō 1). The shrine was founded as a subsidiary of the Inner Shrine in 1923 (Taishō 12), following national approval in 1919 (Taishō 8).
During the 1945 (Shōwa 20) Ujiyamada air raids, the shukueiya was burned, but the main structure remained intact.
== deity == *Main deity: [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] (やまとひめのみこと)
== Grounds ==
Main Hall: Modeled after the Inner Shrine with [[Chigi]] and [[Katsuogi]], featuring a [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architectural style with thatched roofing and facing south.
Shukueiya: Provides blessings and talismans.
== Festivals == Festivals follow the Inner Shrine’s schedule, including:
[[Saitan-sai]] (January 1): Celebrating the new year and imperial lineage.
[[Kigensetsu]] (February 11): Commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s founding of the nation.
[[Kinen-sai]] (February 20): Praying for bountiful harvests.
[[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (October 24-25): Offering the year’s harvest to Amaterasu.
[[Niinamesai]] (November 26): Thanking the gods for the harvest.
== Worshipers == The Yamato-hime Shrine Reverence Association, founded in 1948 (Shōwa 23), holds regular events, including biannual festivals in May and November.
== Access == *Nearest station: [[Kintetsu]] [[Toba Line]], [[Isuzugawa Station]], 10 minutes on foot. *Parking: Available at the adjacent Jingu Chōkokan museum.

{{Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamatohime-no-miya, shaden-2.jpg|256px|Yamatohime-no-miya]] | name=Yamatohime-no-miya | location=[[Mie Prefecture]], [[Ise City]], Kusube-cho, Akaidani 5[http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/shrine/area09.php "Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan | 125 Shrines 5"] (Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan, accessed May 3, 2015) | deity=[[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] | Rank=Betto Shrine of the [[Kōdaijingū|Inner Shrine]] | Foundation=[[1923]] (Taishō 12) | Main Structure=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates={{Coord|34|29|9.2|N|136|43|22.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Festival= }} '''Yamatohime-no-miya''' (倭姫宮) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Ise City]], Kusube-cho. It is a subsidiary shrine ("betsugu") of the [[Kōtai Jingu (Inner Shrine)|Inner Shrine]] of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]].
== Overview ==
In [[Ise Jingu]], there are 14 [[Betsugu]] (associated shrines) in addition to the [[Inner Shrine]] (Naiku) and the [[Outer Shrine]] (Geku). These include {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月讀宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|2=es|3=Tsukiyomi no miya (Naikū)|4=ja|5=月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyominomiya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyominomiya|qq=}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takihara-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Izawa-no-Miya|ja|伊雑宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izawa-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kazebi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazebi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}}, and others. Among them, the creation date is clearly documented only for Yamatohime-no-Miya (倭姫宮), which was established on [[November 5, 1923]] (Taisho 12), making it the newest of the shrines.
The enshrined deity is {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=}}, the fourth imperial princess of the 11th emperor, [[Emperor Suinin]], as told in [[Japanese mythology]].
Like other outlying Betsugu shrines, the site has a Shukueiya (宿衛屋), a lodging for [[Shinto priest]]s who are stationed during visiting hours. They manage the distribution of talismans and amulets, as well as arrangements for [[kagura]] and ritual offerings.
=== location === Adjacent to Kuratayama (倉田山) lies "Ainoyama" (間の山), home to a small [[kofun]] (ancient tomb) called Onoue-no-Goryo (尾上御陵). This tomb is traditionally believed to be the burial site of Yamatohime-no-Mikoto. In honor of this connection, the temple town at the base of 常明寺 (Jōmyō-ji) was renamed '''Yamato Town''' (倭町, Yamatomachi) in [[1868]] (Meiji 1). There are conflicting theories about the tomb's shape, with some identifying it as a circular tomb ([[enzuka]]) and others as a keyhole-shaped mound ([[zenpō-kōen-fun]]).
In [[1886]] (Meiji 19), the residents of Ujiyamada Town formed the {{ill|Shin’enkai|ja|神苑会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin’enkai|qq=}}, a [[foundation]] focused on preventing fires from spreading to the shrine and preserving the shrine's aesthetic and cleanliness. In [[1891]] (Meiji 24), the Shin’enkai established the '''Jingu Agricultural Museum''' (神宮農業館), Japan’s oldest [[industrial museum]], near the Outer Shrine. In [[1905]] (Meiji 38), the museum was relocated to Kuratayama.
During [[Emperor Meiji]]'s [[Imperial visit]] to Ise Jingu, road improvements were necessary to accommodate [[automobiles]]. A road passing through Kuratayama was completed in [[1910]] (Meiji 43) and named '''Gokō-dōri''' (御幸通り, Imperial Road). The following year, in [[1911]] (Meiji 44), the Shin’enkai completed the {{ill|Jingu Chokokan|ja|神宮徴古館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Chokokan|qq=}}, which featured a Versailles-inspired front garden. The Chokokan included a state guest room used for resting by dignitaries such as [[Emperor Taisho]]. Further development in Kuratayama as an educational district followed the relocation of Jingu Kōgakkan (now {{ill|Kōgakkan University|ja|皇學館大学|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōgakkan University|qq=}}) to the area in [[1919]] (Taisho 8).
Due to the proximity of Onoue-no-Goryo, Yamatohime-no-Miya was constructed on a cleared section of Kuratayama. In [[1928]] (Showa 3), the tomb was officially designated as the '''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Burial Reference Site''' (倭姫命御陵墓参考地) and placed under the management of the [[Imperial Household Agency]]. == History == [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] is credited with establishing the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], creating rituals, and setting the foundation of [[Shinto]] practices. However, no shrine dedicated to her existed before the Meiji period.
Calls for a shrine arose around 1887 (Meiji 20), culminating in its official approval in 1912 (Taishō 1). The shrine was founded as a subsidiary of the Inner Shrine in 1923 (Taishō 12), following national approval in 1919 (Taishō 8).
During the 1945 (Shōwa 20) Ujiyamada air raids, the shukueiya was burned, but the main structure remained intact.

== Grounds ==
Main Hall: Modeled after the Inner Shrine with [[Chigi]] and [[Katsuogi]], featuring a [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architectural style with thatched roofing and facing south.
Shukueiya: Provides blessings and talismans.
== Festivals == Festivals follow the Inner Shrine’s schedule, including:
[[Saitan-sai]] (January 1): Celebrating the new year and imperial lineage.
[[Kigensetsu]] (February 11): Commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s founding of the nation.
[[Kinen-sai]] (February 20): Praying for bountiful harvests.
[[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (October 24-25): Offering the year’s harvest to Amaterasu.
[[Niinamesai]] (November 26): Thanking the gods for the harvest.

{{Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamatohime-no-miya, shaden-2.jpg|256px|Yamatohime-no-miya]] | name=Yamatohime-no-miya | location=[[Mie Prefecture]], [[Ise City]], Kusube-cho, Akaidani 5[http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/shrine/area09.php "Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan | 125 Shrines 5"] (Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan, accessed May 3, 2015) | deity=[[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] | Rank=Betto Shrine of the [[Kōdaijingū|Inner Shrine]] | Foundation=[[1923]] (Taishō 12) | Main Structure=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates={{Coord|34|29|9.2|N|136|43|22.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Festival= }} '''Yamatohime-no-miya''' (倭姫宮) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Ise City]], Kusube-cho. It is a subsidiary shrine ("betsugu") of the [[Kōtai Jingu (Inner Shrine)|Inner Shrine]] of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]].
== Overview ==
In [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]], there are 14 [[Betsugu]] (associated shrines) in addition to the [[Inner Shrine]] (Naiku) and the [[Outer Shrine]] (Geku). These include {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月讀宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|2=es|3=Tsukiyomi no miya (Naikū)|4=ja|5=月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyominomiya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyominomiya|qq=}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takihara-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Izawa-no-miya|lt=Izawa-no-miya|lt=Izawa-no-Miya|ja|伊雑宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izawa-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kazebi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazebi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}}, and others. Among them, the creation date is clearly documented only for Yamatohime-no-Miya (倭姫宮), which was established on [[November 5, 1923]] (Taisho 12), making it the newest of the shrines.
The enshrined deity is {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=}}, the fourth imperial princess of the 11th emperor, [[Emperor Suinin]], as told in [[Japanese mythology]].
Like other outlying Betsugu shrines, the site has a Shukueiya (宿衛屋), a lodging for [[Shinto priest]]s who are stationed during visiting hours. They manage the distribution of talismans and amulets, as well as arrangements for [[kagura]] and ritual offerings.
=== location === Adjacent to Kuratayama (倉田山) lies "Ainoyama" (間の山), home to a small [[kofun]] (ancient tomb) called Onoue-no-Goryo (尾上御陵). This tomb is traditionally believed to be the burial site of Yamatohime-no-Mikoto. In honor of this connection, the temple town at the base of 常明寺 (Jōmyō-ji) was renamed '''Yamato Town''' (倭町, Yamatomachi) in [[1868]] (Meiji 1). There are conflicting theories about the tomb's shape, with some identifying it as a circular tomb ([[enzuka]]) and others as a keyhole-shaped mound ([[zenpō-kōen-fun]]).
In [[1886]] (Meiji 19), the residents of Ujiyamada Town formed the {{ill|Shin’enkai|ja|神苑会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin’enkai|qq=}}, a [[foundation]] focused on preventing fires from spreading to the shrine and preserving the shrine's aesthetic and cleanliness. In [[1891]] (Meiji 24), the Shin’enkai established the '''Jingu Agricultural Museum''' (神宮農業館), Japan’s oldest [[industrial museum]], near the Outer Shrine. In [[1905]] (Meiji 38), the museum was relocated to Kuratayama.
During [[Emperor Meiji]]'s [[Imperial visit]] to Ise Jingu, road improvements were necessary to accommodate [[automobiles]]. A road passing through Kuratayama was completed in [[1910]] (Meiji 43) and named '''Gokō-dōri''' (御幸通り, Imperial Road). The following year, in [[1911]] (Meiji 44), the Shin’enkai completed the {{ill|Jingu Chokokan|ja|神宮徴古館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Chokokan|qq=}}, which featured a Versailles-inspired front garden. The Chokokan included a state guest room used for resting by dignitaries such as [[Emperor Taisho]]. Further development in Kuratayama as an educational district followed the relocation of Jingu Kōgakkan (now {{ill|Kōgakkan University|ja|皇學館大学|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōgakkan University|qq=}}) to the area in [[1919]] (Taisho 8).
Due to the proximity of Onoue-no-Goryo, Yamatohime-no-Miya was constructed on a cleared section of Kuratayama. In [[1928]] (Showa 3), the tomb was officially designated as the '''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Burial Reference Site''' (倭姫命御陵墓参考地) and placed under the management of the [[Imperial Household Agency]]. == History == [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] is credited with establishing the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], creating rituals, and setting the foundation of [[Shinto]] practices. However, no shrine dedicated to her existed before the Meiji period.
Calls for a shrine arose around 1887 (Meiji 20), culminating in its official approval in 1912 (Taishō 1). The shrine was founded as a subsidiary of the Inner Shrine in 1923 (Taishō 12), following national approval in 1919 (Taishō 8).
During the 1945 (Shōwa 20) Ujiyamada air raids, the shukueiya was burned, but the main structure remained intact.
== deity == *Main deity: [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] (やまとひめのみこと)
== Grounds ==
Main Hall: Modeled after the Inner Shrine with [[Chigi]] and [[Katsuogi]], featuring a [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architectural style with thatched roofing and facing south.
Shukueiya: Provides blessings and talismans.
== Festivals == Festivals follow the Inner Shrine’s schedule, including:
[[Saitan-sai]] (January 1): Celebrating the new year and imperial lineage.
[[Kigensetsu]] (February 11): Commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s founding of the nation.
[[Kinen-sai]] (February 20): Praying for bountiful harvests.
[[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (October 24-25): Offering the year’s harvest to Amaterasu.
[[Niinamesai]] (November 26): Thanking the gods for the harvest.
== Worshipers == The Yamato-hime Shrine Reverence Association, founded in 1948 (Shōwa 23), holds regular events, including biannual festivals in May and November.
== Access == *Nearest station: [[Kintetsu]] [[Toba Line]], [[Isuzugawa Station]], 10 minutes on foot. *Parking: Available at the adjacent Jingu Chōkokan museum.

[[Saitan-sai]] (January 1): Celebrating the new year and imperial lineage.
[[Kigensetsu]] (February 11): Commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s founding of the nation.
[[Kinen-sai]] (February 20): Praying for bountiful harvests.
[[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (October 24-25): Offering the year’s harvest to Amaterasu.
[[Niinamesai]] (November 26): Thanking the gods for the harvest.
== Worshipers == The Yamato-hime Shrine Reverence Association, founded in 1948 (Shōwa 23), holds regular events, including biannual festivals in May and November.
== Access == *Nearest station: [[Kintetsu]] [[Toba Line]], [[Isuzugawa Station]], 10 minutes on foot. *Parking: Available at the adjacent Jingu Chōkokan museum.

{{Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Yamatohime-no-miya, shaden-2.jpg|256px|Yamatohime-no-miya]] | name=Yamatohime-no-miya | location=[[Mie Prefecture]], [[Ise City]], Kusube-cho, Akaidani 5[http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/shrine/area09.php "Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan | 125 Shrines 5"] (Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan, accessed May 3, 2015) | deity=[[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] | Rank=Betto Shrine of the [[Kōdaijingū|Inner Shrine]] | Foundation=[[1923]] (Taishō 12) | Main Structure=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates={{Coord|34|29|9.2|N|136|43|22.2|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Festival= }} '''Yamatohime-no-miya''' (倭姫宮) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Mie Prefecture]], [[Ise City]], Kusube-cho. It is a subsidiary shrine ("betsugu") of the [[Kōtai Jingu (Inner Shrine)|Inner Shrine]] of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]].
== Overview ==
In [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]], there are 14 [[Betsugu]] (associated shrines) in addition to the [[Inner Shrine]] (Naiku) and the [[Outer Shrine]] (Geku). These include {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月讀宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|2=es|3=Tsukiyomi no miya (Naikū)|4=ja|5=月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyominomiya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyominomiya|qq=}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takihara-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Izawa-no-miya|lt=Izawa-no-miya|lt=Izawa-no-Miya|ja|伊雑宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izawa-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|lt=Kazahinomi-no-Miya|lt=Kazebi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazebi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}}, and others. Among them, the creation date is clearly documented only for Yamatohime-no-Miya (倭姫宮), which was established on [[November 5, 1923]] (Taisho 12), making it the newest of the shrines.
The enshrined deity is {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=}}, the fourth imperial princess of the 11th emperor, [[Emperor Suinin]], as told in [[Japanese mythology]].
Like other outlying Betsugu shrines, the site has a Shukueiya (宿衛屋), a lodging for [[Shinto priest]]s who are stationed during visiting hours. They manage the distribution of talismans and amulets, as well as arrangements for [[kagura]] and ritual offerings.
=== location === Adjacent to Kuratayama (倉田山) lies "Ainoyama" (間の山), home to a small [[kofun]] (ancient tomb) called Onoue-no-Goryo (尾上御陵). This tomb is traditionally believed to be the burial site of Yamatohime-no-Mikoto. In honor of this connection, the temple town at the base of 常明寺 (Jōmyō-ji) was renamed '''Yamato Town''' (倭町, Yamatomachi) in [[1868]] (Meiji 1). There are conflicting theories about the tomb's shape, with some identifying it as a circular tomb ([[enzuka]]) and others as a keyhole-shaped mound ([[zenpō-kōen-fun]]).
In [[1886]] (Meiji 19), the residents of Ujiyamada Town formed the {{ill|Shin’enkai|ja|神苑会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin’enkai|qq=}}, a [[foundation]] focused on preventing fires from spreading to the shrine and preserving the shrine's aesthetic and cleanliness. In [[1891]] (Meiji 24), the Shin’enkai established the '''Jingu Agricultural Museum''' (神宮農業館), Japan’s oldest [[industrial museum]], near the Outer Shrine. In [[1905]] (Meiji 38), the museum was relocated to Kuratayama.
During [[Emperor Meiji]]'s [[Imperial visit]] to Ise Jingu, road improvements were necessary to accommodate [[automobiles]]. A road passing through Kuratayama was completed in [[1910]] (Meiji 43) and named '''Gokō-dōri''' (御幸通り, Imperial Road). The following year, in [[1911]] (Meiji 44), the Shin’enkai completed the {{ill|Jingu Chokokan|ja|神宮徴古館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Chokokan|qq=}}, which featured a Versailles-inspired front garden. The Chokokan included a state guest room used for resting by dignitaries such as [[Emperor Taisho]]. Further development in Kuratayama as an educational district followed the relocation of Jingu Kōgakkan (now {{ill|Kōgakkan University|ja|皇學館大学|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōgakkan University|qq=}}) to the area in [[1919]] (Taisho 8).
Due to the proximity of Onoue-no-Goryo, Yamatohime-no-Miya was constructed on a cleared section of Kuratayama. In [[1928]] (Showa 3), the tomb was officially designated as the '''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Burial Reference Site''' (倭姫命御陵墓参考地) and placed under the management of the [[Imperial Household Agency]]. == History == [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] is credited with establishing the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], creating rituals, and setting the foundation of [[Shinto]] practices. However, no shrine dedicated to her existed before the Meiji period.
Calls for a shrine arose around 1887 (Meiji 20), culminating in its official approval in 1912 (Taishō 1). The shrine was founded as a subsidiary of the Inner Shrine in 1923 (Taishō 12), following national approval in 1919 (Taishō 8).
During the 1945 (Shōwa 20) Ujiyamada air raids, the shukueiya was burned, but the main structure remained intact.
== deity == *Main deity: [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] (やまとひめのみこと)
== Grounds ==
Main Hall: Modeled after the Inner Shrine with [[Chigi]] and [[Katsuogi]], featuring a [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architectural style with thatched roofing and facing south.
Shukueiya: Provides blessings and talismans.
== Festivals == Festivals follow the Inner Shrine’s schedule, including:
[[Saitan-sai]] (January 1): Celebrating the new year and imperial lineage.
[[Kigensetsu]] (February 11): Commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s founding of the nation.
[[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] (February 20): Praying for bountiful harvests.
[[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (October 24-25): Offering the year’s harvest to Amaterasu.
[[Niinamesai]] (November 26): Thanking the gods for the harvest.
== Worshipers == The Yamato-hime Shrine Reverence Association, founded in 1948 (Shōwa 23), holds regular events, including biannual festivals in May and November.
== Access == *Nearest station: [[Kintetsu]] [[Toba Line]], [[Isuzugawa Station]], 10 minutes on foot. *Parking: Available at the adjacent Jingu Chōkokan museum.

Among them, the creation date is clearly documented only for Yamatohime-no-Miya (倭姫宮), which was established on [[November 5, 1923]] (Taisho 12), making it the newest of the shrines.
The enshrined deity is {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|ja|倭姫命|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=}}, the fourth imperial princess of the 11th emperor, [[Emperor Suinin]], as told in [[Japanese mythology]].
Like other outlying Betsugu shrines, the site has a Shukueiya (宿衛屋), a lodging for [[Shinto priest]]s who are stationed during visiting hours. They manage the distribution of talismans and amulets, as well as arrangements for [[kagura]] and ritual offerings.
=== location === Adjacent to Kuratayama (倉田山) lies "Ainoyama" (間の山), home to a small [[kofun]] (ancient tomb) called Onoue-no-Goryo (尾上御陵). This tomb is traditionally believed to be the burial site of Yamatohime-no-Mikoto. In honor of this connection, the temple town at the base of 常明寺 (Jōmyō-ji) was renamed '''Yamato Town''' (倭町, Yamatomachi) in [[1868]] (Meiji 1). There are conflicting theories about the tomb's shape, with some identifying it as a circular tomb ([[enzuka]]) and others as a keyhole-shaped mound ([[zenpō-kōen-fun]]).
In [[1886]] (Meiji 19), the residents of Ujiyamada Town formed the {{ill|Shin’enkai|ja|神苑会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin’enkai|qq=}}, a [[foundation]] focused on preventing fires from spreading to the shrine and preserving the shrine's aesthetic and cleanliness. In [[1891]] (Meiji 24), the Shin’enkai established the '''Jingu Agricultural Museum''' (神宮農業館), Japan’s oldest [[industrial museum]], near the Outer Shrine. In [[1905]] (Meiji 38), the museum was relocated to Kuratayama.
During [[Emperor Meiji]]'s [[Imperial visit]] to Ise Jingu, road improvements were necessary to accommodate [[automobiles]]. A road passing through Kuratayama was completed in [[1910]] (Meiji 43) and named '''Gokō-dōri''' (御幸通り, Imperial Road). The following year, in [[1911]] (Meiji 44), the Shin’enkai completed the {{ill|Jingu Chokokan|ja|神宮徴古館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Chokokan|qq=}}, which featured a Versailles-inspired front garden. The Chokokan included a state guest room used for resting by dignitaries such as [[Emperor Taisho]]. Further development in Kuratayama as an educational district followed the relocation of Jingu Kōgakkan (now {{ill|Kōgakkan University|ja|皇學館大学|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōgakkan University|qq=}}) to the area in [[1919]] (Taisho 8).
Due to the proximity of Onoue-no-Goryo, Yamatohime-no-Miya was constructed on a cleared section of Kuratayama. In [[1928]] (Showa 3), the tomb was officially designated as the '''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Burial Reference Site''' (倭姫命御陵墓参考地) and placed under the management of the [[Imperial Household Agency]]. == History == [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] is credited with establishing the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], creating rituals, and setting the foundation of [[Shinto]] practices. However, no shrine dedicated to her existed before the Meiji period.
Calls for a shrine arose around 1887 (Meiji 20), culminating in its official approval in 1912 (Taishō 1). The shrine was founded as a subsidiary of the Inner Shrine in 1923 (Taishō 12), following national approval in 1919 (Taishō 8).
During the 1945 (Shōwa 20) Ujiyamada air raids, the shukueiya was burned, but the main structure remained intact.
== deity == *Main deity: [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] (やまとひめのみこと)
== Grounds ==
Main Hall: Modeled after the Inner Shrine with [[Chigi]] and [[Katsuogi]], featuring a [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architectural style with thatched roofing and facing south.
Shukueiya: Provides blessings and talismans.
== Festivals == Festivals follow the Inner Shrine’s schedule, including:
[[Saitan-sai]] (January 1): Celebrating the new year and imperial lineage.
[[Kigensetsu]] (February 11): Commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s founding of the nation.
[[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] (February 20): Praying for bountiful harvests.
[[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (October 24-25): Offering the year’s harvest to Amaterasu.
[[Niinamesai]] (November 26): Thanking the gods for the harvest.
== Worshipers == The Yamato-hime Shrine Reverence Association, founded in 1948 (Shōwa 23), holds regular events, including biannual festivals in May and November.
== Access == *Nearest station: [[Kintetsu]] [[Toba Line]], [[Isuzugawa Station]], 10 minutes on foot. *Parking: Available at the adjacent Jingu Chōkokan museum.
