{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11387081]] Wikidata name: Yamatohime-no-miya [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Yamatohime-no-miya 01.JPG | name=Yamatohime-no-miya | location=[[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], Kusube-cho, Akaidani 5[http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/shrine/area09.php "Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan | 125 Shrines 5"] (Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan, accessed May 3, 2015) | deity={{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qid=Q4388732}} | Rank=Betto Shrine of the [[Kōdaijingū|Inner Shrine]] | Foundation=[[1923]] (Taishō 12) | Main Structure=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates={{Coord|34.48583|N|136.72306|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Festival= | prefecture={{ill|Ise|qid=Q328067}} | architecture_style={{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545}} }} '''Yamatohime-no-miya''' (倭姫宮) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], Kusube-cho. It is a subsidiary shrine ("betsugu") of the [[Kōtai Jingū|Inner Shrine]] of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]].
== Overview == [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_02.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii gate at the Omotesando entrance]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_03.JPG|thumb|200px|Chōzuya (purification fountain)]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_04.JPG|thumb|200px|Stone steps along the approach]] In [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]], there are 14 [[:en:Betsugu|Betsugu]] (associated shrines) in addition to the [[Kōtai Jingū|Inner Shrine]] (Naiku) and the [[Kōtai Jingū|Outer Shrine]] (Geku). These include {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月讀宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|2=es|3=Tsukiyomi no miya (Naikū)|4=ja|5=月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyominomiya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyominomiya|qq=}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takihara-no-Miya|qq=}}, [[:en:Izawa-no-miya|Izawa-no-Miya]], {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|lt=Kazahinomi-no-Miya|lt=Kazebi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazebi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}}, and others. Among them, the creation date is clearly documented only for Yamatohime-no-Miya (倭姫宮), which was established on [[November 5, 1923]] (Taisho 12), making it the newest of the shrines.
The enshrined deity is [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]], the fourth imperial princess of the 11th emperor, [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], as told in [[Japanese mythology]].
Like other outlying Betsugu shrines, the site has a Shukueiya (宿衛屋), a lodging for [[:en:Shinto priest|Shinto priest]]s who are stationed during visiting hours. They manage the distribution of talismans and amulets, as well as arrangements for [[kagura]] and ritual offerings.
=== location === Adjacent to Kuratayama (倉田山) lies "Ainoyama" (間の山), home to a small [[kofun]] (ancient tomb) called Onoue-no-Goryo (尾上御陵). This tomb is traditionally believed to be the burial site of Yamatohime-no-Mikoto. In honor of this connection, the temple town at the base of 常明寺 (Jōmyō-ji) was renamed '''Yamato Town''' (倭町, Yamatomachi) in [[:en:1868|1868]] (Meiji 1). There are conflicting theories about the tomb's shape, with some identifying it as a circular tomb ([[enzuka]]) and others as a keyhole-shaped mound ([[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]).
In [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19), the residents of Ujiyamada Town formed the {{ill|Shin’enkai|ja|神苑会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin’enkai|qq=|1=Shin'enkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590780}}, a [[:en:Foundation|Foundation]] focused on preventing fires from spreading to the shrine and preserving the shrine's aesthetic and cleanliness. In [[:en:1891|1891]] (Meiji 24), the Shin’enkai established the '''Jingu Agricultural Museum''' (神宮農業館), Japan’s oldest [[industrial museum]], near the Outer Shrine. In [[:en:1905|1905]] (Meiji 38), the museum was relocated to Kuratayama.
During [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]]'s [[Imperial visit]] to Ise Jingu, road improvements were necessary to accommodate [[:en:automobiles|automobiles]]. A road passing through Kuratayama was completed in [[:en:1910|1910]] (Meiji 43) and named '''Gokō-dōri''' (御幸通り, Imperial Road). The following year, in [[:en:1911|1911]] (Meiji 44), the Shin’enkai completed the {{ill|Jingu Chokokan|ja|神宮徴古館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Chokokan|qq=|1=Jingu Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589488}}, which featured a Versailles-inspired front garden. The Chokokan included a state guest room used for resting by dignitaries such as [[:en:Emperor Taisho|Emperor Taisho]]. Further development in Kuratayama as an educational district followed the relocation of Jingu Kōgakkan (now [[:en:Kogakkan University|Kōgakkan University]]) to the area in [[:en:1919|1919]] (Taisho 8).
Due to the proximity of Onoue-no-Goryo, Yamatohime-no-Miya was constructed on a cleared section of Kuratayama. In [[:en:1928|1928]] (Showa 3), the tomb was officially designated as the '''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Burial Reference Site''' (倭姫命御陵墓参考地) and placed under the management of the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]]. == History == [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] is credited with establishing the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], creating rituals, and setting the foundation of [[Shinto]] practices. However, no shrine dedicated to her existed before the Meiji period.
Calls for a shrine arose around 1887 (Meiji 20), culminating in its official approval in 1912 (Taishō 1). The shrine was founded as a subsidiary of the Inner Shrine in 1923 (Taishō 12), following national approval in 1919 (Taishō 8).
During the 1945 (Shōwa 20) Ujiyamada air raids, the shukueiya was burned, but the main structure remained intact.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Yamatohime-no-miya (Naiku)]][[ja:倭姫宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11387081]] Wikidata name: Yamatohime-no-miya [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Yamatohime-no-miya 01.JPG | name=Yamatohime-no-miya | location=[[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], Kusube-cho, Akaidani 5[http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/shrine/area09.php "Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan | 125 Shrines 5"] (Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan, accessed May 3, 2015) | deity={{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qid=Q4388732}} | Rank=Betto Shrine of the [[Kōdaijingū|Inner Shrine]] | Foundation=[[1923]] (Taishō 12) | Main Structure=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates={{Coord|34.48583|N|136.72306|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Festival= | prefecture={{ill|Ise|qid=Q328067}} | architecture_style={{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|qid=Q930545}} }} '''Yamatohime-no-miya''' (倭姫宮) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], Kusube-cho. It is a subsidiary shrine ("betsugu") of the [[Kōtai Jingū|Inner Shrine]] of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]].
== Overview == [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_02.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii gate at the Omotesando entrance]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_03.JPG|thumb|200px|Chōzuya (purification fountain)]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_04.JPG|thumb|200px|Stone steps along the approach]] In [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]], there are 14 [[:en:Betsugu|Betsugu]] (associated shrines) in addition to the [[Kōtai Jingū|Inner Shrine]] (Naiku) and the [[Kōtai Jingū|Outer Shrine]] (Geku). These include {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月讀宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|2=es|3=Tsukiyomi no miya (Naikū)|4=ja|5=月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyominomiya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyominomiya|qq=}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takihara-no-Miya|qq=}}, [[:en:Izawa-no-miya|Izawa-no-Miya]], {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|lt=Kazahinomi-no-Miya|lt=Kazebi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazebi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}}, and others. Among them, the creation date is clearly documented only for Yamatohime-no-Miya (倭姫宮), which was established on [[November 5, 1923]] (Taisho 12), making it the newest of the shrines.
The enshrined deity is [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]], the fourth imperial princess of the 11th emperor, [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], as told in [[Japanese mythology]].
Like other outlying Betsugu shrines, the site has a Shukueiya (宿衛屋), a lodging for [[:en:Shinto priest|Shinto priest]]s who are stationed during visiting hours. They manage the distribution of talismans and amulets, as well as arrangements for [[kagura]] and ritual offerings.
=== location === Adjacent to Kuratayama (倉田山) lies "Ainoyama" (間の山), home to a small [[kofun]] (ancient tomb) called Onoue-no-Goryo (尾上御陵). This tomb is traditionally believed to be the burial site of Yamatohime-no-Mikoto. In honor of this connection, the temple town at the base of 常明寺 (Jōmyō-ji) was renamed '''Yamato Town''' (倭町, Yamatomachi) in [[:en:1868|1868]] (Meiji 1). There are conflicting theories about the tomb's shape, with some identifying it as a circular tomb ([[enzuka]]) and others as a keyhole-shaped mound ([[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]).
In [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19), the residents of Ujiyamada Town formed the {{ill|Shin’enkai|ja|神苑会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin’enkai|qq=|1=Shin'enkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590780}}, a [[:en:Foundation|Foundation]] focused on preventing fires from spreading to the shrine and preserving the shrine's aesthetic and cleanliness. In [[:en:1891|1891]] (Meiji 24), the Shin’enkai established the '''Jingu Agricultural Museum''' (神宮農業館), Japan’s oldest [[industrial museum]], near the Outer Shrine. In [[:en:1905|1905]] (Meiji 38), the museum was relocated to Kuratayama.
During [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]]'s [[Imperial visit]] to Ise Jingu, road improvements were necessary to accommodate [[:en:automobiles|automobiles]]. A road passing through Kuratayama was completed in [[:en:1910|1910]] (Meiji 43) and named '''Gokō-dōri''' (御幸通り, Imperial Road). The following year, in [[:en:1911|1911]] (Meiji 44), the Shin’enkai completed the {{ill|Jingu Chokokan|ja|神宮徴古館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Chokokan|qq=|1=Jingu Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589488}}, which featured a Versailles-inspired front garden. The Chokokan included a state guest room used for resting by dignitaries such as [[:en:Emperor Taisho|Emperor Taisho]]. Further development in Kuratayama as an educational district followed the relocation of Jingu Kōgakkan (now [[:en:Kogakkan University|Kōgakkan University]]) to the area in [[:en:1919|1919]] (Taisho 8).
Due to the proximity of Onoue-no-Goryo, Yamatohime-no-Miya was constructed on a cleared section of Kuratayama. In [[:en:1928|1928]] (Showa 3), the tomb was officially designated as the '''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Burial Reference Site''' (倭姫命御陵墓参考地) and placed under the management of the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]]. == History == [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] is credited with establishing the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], creating rituals, and setting the foundation of [[Shinto]] practices. However, no shrine dedicated to her existed before the Meiji period.
Calls for a shrine arose around 1887 (Meiji 20), culminating in its official approval in 1912 (Taishō 1). The shrine was founded as a subsidiary of the Inner Shrine in 1923 (Taishō 12), following national approval in 1919 (Taishō 8).
During the 1945 (Shōwa 20) Ujiyamada air raids, the shukueiya was burned, but the main structure remained intact.
== deity == *Main deity: [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] (やまとひめのみこと)
== Grounds ==
Main Hall: Modeled after the Inner Shrine with [[:en:Chigi|Chigi]] and [[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]], featuring a [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architectural style with thatched roofing and facing south.
Shukueiya: Provides blessings and talismans.
== Festivals == Festivals follow the Inner Shrine’s schedule, including:
[[Saitan-sai]] (January 1): Celebrating the new year and imperial lineage.
[[Kigensetsu]] (February 11): Commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s founding of the nation.
[[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] (February 20): Praying for bountiful harvests.
[[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (October 24-25): Offering the year’s harvest to Amaterasu.
[[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] (November 26): Thanking the gods for the harvest.
== Worshipers == The Yamato-hime Shrine Reverence Association, founded in 1948 (Shōwa 23), holds regular events, including biannual festivals in May and November.
== Access == *Nearest station: [[:en:Kintetsu|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Toba Line|Toba Line]], [[:en:Isuzugawa Station|Isuzugawa Station]], 10 minutes on foot. *Parking: Available at the adjacent Jingu Chōkokan museum.

In [[:en:1905|1905]] (Meiji 38), the museum was relocated to Kuratayama.

During the 1945 (Shōwa 20) Ujiyamada air raids, the shukueiya was burned, but the main structure remained intact.
== deity == *Main deity: [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] (やまとひめのみこと)
== Grounds ==
Main Hall: Modeled after the Inner Shrine with [[:en:Chigi|Chigi]] and [[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]], featuring a [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architectural style with thatched roofing and facing south.
Shukueiya: Provides blessings and talismans.
== Festivals == Festivals follow the Inner Shrine’s schedule, including:
[[Saitan-sai]] (January 1): Celebrating the new year and imperial lineage.
[[Kigensetsu]] (February 11): Commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s founding of the nation.
[[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] (February 20): Praying for bountiful harvests.
[[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (October 24-25): Offering the year’s harvest to Amaterasu.
[[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] (November 26): Thanking the gods for the harvest.
== Worshipers == The Yamato-hime Shrine Reverence Association, founded in 1948 (Shōwa 23), holds regular events, including biannual festivals in May and November.

It is a subsidiary shrine ("betsugu") of the [[Kōtai Jingū|Inner Shrine]] of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]].
== Overview == [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_02.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii gate at the Omotesando entrance]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_03.JPG|thumb|200px|Chōzuya (purification fountain)]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_04.JPG|thumb|200px|Stone steps along the approach]] In [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]], there are 14 [[:en:Betsugu|Betsugu]] (associated shrines) in addition to the [[Kōtai Jingū|Inner Shrine]] (Naiku) and the [[Kōtai Jingū|Outer Shrine]] (Geku). These include {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月讀宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|2=es|3=Tsukiyomi no miya (Naikū)|4=ja|5=月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyominomiya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyominomiya|qq=}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takihara-no-Miya|qq=}}, [[:en:Izawa-no-miya|Izawa-no-Miya]], {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|lt=Kazahinomi-no-Miya|lt=Kazebi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazebi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}}, and others. Among them, the creation date is clearly documented only for Yamatohime-no-Miya (倭姫宮), which was established on [[November 5, 1923]] (Taisho 12), making it the newest of the shrines.
The enshrined deity is [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]], the fourth imperial princess of the 11th emperor, [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], as told in [[Japanese mythology]].
Like other outlying Betsugu shrines, the site has a Shukueiya (宿衛屋), a lodging for [[:en:Shinto priest|Shinto priest]]s who are stationed during visiting hours. They manage the distribution of talismans and amulets, as well as arrangements for [[kagura]] and ritual offerings.
=== location === Adjacent to Kuratayama (倉田山) lies "Ainoyama" (間の山), home to a small [[kofun]] (ancient tomb) called Onoue-no-Goryo (尾上御陵). This tomb is traditionally believed to be the burial site of Yamatohime-no-Mikoto. In honor of this connection, the temple town at the base of 常明寺 (Jōmyō-ji) was renamed '''Yamato Town''' (倭町, Yamatomachi) in [[:en:1868|1868]] (Meiji 1). There are conflicting theories about the tomb's shape, with some identifying it as a circular tomb ([[enzuka]]) and others as a keyhole-shaped mound ([[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]).
In [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19), the residents of Ujiyamada Town formed the {{ill|Shin’enkai|ja|神苑会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin’enkai|qq=|1=Shin'enkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590780}}, a [[:en:Foundation|Foundation]] focused on preventing fires from spreading to the shrine and preserving the shrine's aesthetic and cleanliness. In [[:en:1891|1891]] (Meiji 24), the Shin’enkai established the '''Jingu Agricultural Museum''' (神宮農業館), Japan’s oldest [[industrial museum]], near the Outer Shrine. In [[:en:1905|1905]] (Meiji 38), the museum was relocated to Kuratayama.
During [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]]'s [[Imperial visit]] to Ise Jingu, road improvements were necessary to accommodate [[:en:automobiles|automobiles]]. A road passing through Kuratayama was completed in [[:en:1910|1910]] (Meiji 43) and named '''Gokō-dōri''' (御幸通り, Imperial Road). The following year, in [[:en:1911|1911]] (Meiji 44), the Shin’enkai completed the {{ill|Jingu Chokokan|ja|神宮徴古館|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Chokokan|qq=|1=Jingu Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589488}}, which featured a Versailles-inspired front garden. The Chokokan included a state guest room used for resting by dignitaries such as [[:en:Emperor Taisho|Emperor Taisho]]. Further development in Kuratayama as an educational district followed the relocation of Jingu Kōgakkan (now [[:en:Kogakkan University|Kōgakkan University]]) to the area in [[:en:1919|1919]] (Taisho 8).
Due to the proximity of Onoue-no-Goryo, Yamatohime-no-Miya was constructed on a cleared section of Kuratayama. In [[:en:1928|1928]] (Showa 3), the tomb was officially designated as the '''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Burial Reference Site''' (倭姫命御陵墓参考地) and placed under the management of the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]]. == History == [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] is credited with establishing the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], creating rituals, and setting the foundation of [[Shinto]] practices. However, no shrine dedicated to her existed before the Meiji period.
Calls for a shrine arose around 1887 (Meiji 20), culminating in its official approval in 1912 (Taishō 1). The shrine was founded as a subsidiary of the Inner Shrine in 1923 (Taishō 12), following national approval in 1919 (Taishō 8).
During the 1945 (Shōwa 20) Ujiyamada air raids, the shukueiya was burned, but the main structure remained intact.
== deity == *Main deity: [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] (やまとひめのみこと)
== Grounds ==
Main Hall: Modeled after the Inner Shrine with [[:en:Chigi|Chigi]] and [[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]], featuring a [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architectural style with thatched roofing and facing south.
Shukueiya: Provides blessings and talismans.
== Festivals == Festivals follow the Inner Shrine’s schedule, including:
[[Saitan-sai]] (January 1): Celebrating the new year and imperial lineage.
[[Kigensetsu]] (February 11): Commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s founding of the nation.
[[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] (February 20): Praying for bountiful harvests.
[[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (October 24-25): Offering the year’s harvest to Amaterasu.
[[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] (November 26): Thanking the gods for the harvest.
== Worshipers == The Yamato-hime Shrine Reverence Association, founded in 1948 (Shōwa 23), holds regular events, including biannual festivals in May and November.
== Access == *Nearest station: [[:en:Kintetsu|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Toba Line|Toba Line]], [[:en:Isuzugawa Station|Isuzugawa Station]], 10 minutes on foot. *Parking: Available at the adjacent Jingu Chōkokan museum.

There are conflicting theories about the tomb's shape, with some identifying it as a circular tomb ([[enzuka]]) and others as a keyhole-shaped mound ([[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]).

In [[:en:1891|1891]] (Meiji 24), the Shin’enkai established the '''Jingu Agricultural Museum''' (神宮農業館), Japan’s oldest [[industrial museum]], near the Outer Shrine. In [[:en:1905|1905]] (Meiji 38), the museum was relocated to Kuratayama.
During [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]]'s [[Imperial visit]] to Ise Jingu, road improvements were necessary to accommodate [[:en:automobiles|automobiles]]. A road passing through Kuratayama was completed in [[:en:1910|1910]] (Meiji 43) and named '''Gokō-dōri''' (御幸通り, Imperial Road). The following year, in [[:en:1911|1911]] (Meiji 44), the Shin’enkai completed the {{ill|Jingu Chokokan|ja|神宮徴古館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Chokokan|1=Jingu Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589488}}, which featured a Versailles-inspired front garden. The Chokokan included a state guest room used for resting by dignitaries such as [[:en:Emperor Taisho|Emperor Taisho]]. Further development in Kuratayama as an educational district followed the relocation of Jingu Kōgakkan (now [[:en:Kogakkan University|Kōgakkan University]]) to the area in [[:en:1919|1919]] (Taisho 8).
Due to the proximity of Onoue-no-Goryo, Yamatohime-no-Miya was constructed on a cleared section of Kuratayama. In [[:en:1928|1928]] (Showa 3), the tomb was officially designated as the '''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Burial Reference Site''' (倭姫命御陵墓参考地) and placed under the management of the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]]. == History == [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] is credited with establishing the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], creating rituals, and setting the foundation of [[Shinto]] practices. However, no shrine dedicated to her existed before the Meiji period.
Calls for a shrine arose around 1887 (Meiji 20), culminating in its official approval in 1912 (Taishō 1). The shrine was founded as a subsidiary of the Inner Shrine in 1923 (Taishō 12), following national approval in 1919 (Taishō 8).
During the 1945 (Shōwa 20) Ujiyamada air raids, the shukueiya was burned, but the main structure remained intact.
== deity == *Main deity: [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] (やまとひめのみこと)
== Grounds ==
Main Hall: Modeled after the Inner Shrine with [[:en:Chigi|Chigi]] and [[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]], featuring a [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architectural style with thatched roofing and facing south.
Shukueiya: Provides blessings and talismans.
== Festivals == Festivals follow the Inner Shrine’s schedule, including:
[[Saitan-sai]] (January 1): Celebrating the new year and imperial lineage.
[[Kigensetsu]] (February 11): Commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s founding of the nation.
[[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] (February 20): Praying for bountiful harvests.
[[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (October 24-25): Offering the year’s harvest to Amaterasu.
[[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] (November 26): Thanking the gods for the harvest.
== Worshipers == The Yamato-hime Shrine Reverence Association, founded in 1948 (Shōwa 23), holds regular events, including biannual festivals in May and November.
== Access == *Nearest station: [[:en:Kintetsu|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Toba Line|Toba Line]], [[:en:Isuzugawa Station|Isuzugawa Station]], 10 minutes on foot. *Parking: Available at the adjacent Jingu Chōkokan museum.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Yamatohime-no-miya (Naiku)]][[ja:倭姫宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11387081]] Wikidata name: Yamatohime-no-miya [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Yamatohime-no-miya 01.JPG | name=Yamatohime-no-miya | location=[[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], Kusube-cho, Akaidani 5[http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/shrine/area09.php "Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan | 125 Shrines 5"] (Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan, accessed May 3, 2015) | deity={{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732}} | Rank=Betto Shrine of the [[Kōdaijingū|Inner Shrine]] | Foundation=[[1923]] (Taishō 12) | Main Structure=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates={{Coord|34.48583|N|136.72306|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Festival= | prefecture={{ill|Ise|WD=Q328067}} | architecture_style={{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545}} }} '''Yamatohime-no-miya''' (倭姫宮) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], Kusube-cho. It is a subsidiary shrine ("betsugu") of the [[Kōtai Jingū|Inner Shrine]] of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]].
== Overview == [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_02.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii gate at the Omotesando entrance]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_03.JPG|thumb|200px|Chōzuya (purification fountain)]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_04.JPG|thumb|200px|Stone steps along the approach]] In [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]], there are 14 [[:en:Betsugu|Betsugu]] (associated shrines) in addition to the [[Kōtai Jingū|Inner Shrine]] (Naiku) and the [[Kōtai Jingū|Outer Shrine]] (Geku). These include {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月讀宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|2=es|3=Tsukiyomi no miya (Naikū)|4=ja|5=月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyominomiya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyominomiya|qq=}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takihara-no-Miya|qq=}}, [[:en:Izawa-no-miya|Izawa-no-Miya]], {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|lt=Kazahinomi-no-Miya|lt=Kazebi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazebi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}}, and others. Among them, the creation date is clearly documented only for Yamatohime-no-Miya (倭姫宮), which was established on [[November 5, 1923]] (Taisho 12), making it the newest of the shrines.
The enshrined deity is [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]], the fourth imperial princess of the 11th emperor, [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], as told in [[Japanese mythology]].
Like other outlying Betsugu shrines, the site has a Shukueiya (宿衛屋), a lodging for [[:en:Shinto priest|Shinto priest]]s who are stationed during visiting hours. They manage the distribution of talismans and amulets, as well as arrangements for [[kagura]] and ritual offerings.
=== location === Adjacent to Kuratayama (倉田山) lies "Ainoyama" (間の山), home to a small [[kofun]] (ancient tomb) called Onoue-no-Goryo (尾上御陵). This tomb is traditionally believed to be the burial site of Yamatohime-no-Mikoto. In honor of this connection, the temple town at the base of 常明寺 (Jōmyō-ji) was renamed '''Yamato Town''' (倭町, Yamatomachi) in [[:en:1868|1868]] (Meiji 1). There are conflicting theories about the tomb's shape, with some identifying it as a circular tomb ([[enzuka]]) and others as a keyhole-shaped mound ([[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]).
In [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19), the residents of Ujiyamada Town formed the {{ill|Shin’enkai|ja|神苑会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin’enkai|1=Shin'enkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590780}}, a [[:en:Foundation|Foundation]] focused on preventing fires from spreading to the shrine and preserving the shrine's aesthetic and cleanliness. In [[:en:1891|1891]] (Meiji 24), the Shin’enkai established the '''Jingu Agricultural Museum''' (神宮農業館), Japan’s oldest [[industrial museum]], near the Outer Shrine. In [[:en:1905|1905]] (Meiji 38), the museum was relocated to Kuratayama.
During [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]]'s [[Imperial visit]] to Ise Jingu, road improvements were necessary to accommodate [[:en:automobiles|automobiles]]. A road passing through Kuratayama was completed in [[:en:1910|1910]] (Meiji 43) and named '''Gokō-dōri''' (御幸通り, Imperial Road). The following year, in [[:en:1911|1911]] (Meiji 44), the Shin’enkai completed the {{ill|Jingu Chokokan|ja|神宮徴古館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Chokokan|1=Jingu Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589488}}, which featured a Versailles-inspired front garden. The Chokokan included a state guest room used for resting by dignitaries such as [[:en:Emperor Taisho|Emperor Taisho]]. Further development in Kuratayama as an educational district followed the relocation of Jingu Kōgakkan (now [[:en:Kogakkan University|Kōgakkan University]]) to the area in [[:en:1919|1919]] (Taisho 8).
Due to the proximity of Onoue-no-Goryo, Yamatohime-no-Miya was constructed on a cleared section of Kuratayama. In [[:en:1928|1928]] (Showa 3), the tomb was officially designated as the '''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Burial Reference Site''' (倭姫命御陵墓参考地) and placed under the management of the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]]. == History == [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] is credited with establishing the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], creating rituals, and setting the foundation of [[Shinto]] practices. However, no shrine dedicated to her existed before the Meiji period.
Calls for a shrine arose around 1887 (Meiji 20), culminating in its official approval in 1912 (Taishō 1). The shrine was founded as a subsidiary of the Inner Shrine in 1923 (Taishō 12), following national approval in 1919 (Taishō 8).
During the 1945 (Shōwa 20) Ujiyamada air raids, the shukueiya was burned, but the main structure remained intact.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Yamatohime-no-miya (Naiku)]][[ja:倭姫宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11387081]] Wikidata name: Yamatohime-no-miya [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Yamatohime-no-miya 01.JPG | name=Yamatohime-no-miya | location=[[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], Kusube-cho, Akaidani 5[http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/shrine/area09.php "Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan | 125 Shrines 5"] (Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan, accessed May 3, 2015) | deity={{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732}} | Rank=Betto Shrine of the [[Kōdaijingū|Inner Shrine]] | Foundation=[[1923]] (Taishō 12) | Main Structure=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates={{Coord|34.48583|N|136.72306|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Festival= | prefecture={{ill|Ise|WD=Q328067}} | architecture_style={{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545}} }} '''Yamatohime-no-miya''' (倭姫宮) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], Kusube-cho. It is a subsidiary shrine ("betsugu") of the [[Kōtai Jingū|Inner Shrine]] of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]].
== Overview == [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_02.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii gate at the Omotesando entrance]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_03.JPG|thumb|200px|Chōzuya (purification fountain)]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_04.JPG|thumb|200px|Stone steps along the approach]] In [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]], there are 14 [[:en:Betsugu|Betsugu]] (associated shrines) in addition to the [[Kōtai Jingū|Inner Shrine]] (Naiku) and the [[Kōtai Jingū|Outer Shrine]] (Geku). These include {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月讀宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|2=es|3=Tsukiyomi no miya (Naikū)|4=ja|5=月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyominomiya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyominomiya|qq=}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takihara-no-Miya|qq=}}, [[:en:Izawa-no-miya|Izawa-no-Miya]], {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|lt=Kazahinomi-no-Miya|lt=Kazebi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazebi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}}, and others. Among them, the creation date is clearly documented only for Yamatohime-no-Miya (倭姫宮), which was established on [[November 5, 1923]] (Taisho 12), making it the newest of the shrines.
The enshrined deity is [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]], the fourth imperial princess of the 11th emperor, [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], as told in [[Japanese mythology]].
Like other outlying Betsugu shrines, the site has a Shukueiya (宿衛屋), a lodging for [[:en:Shinto priest|Shinto priest]]s who are stationed during visiting hours. They manage the distribution of talismans and amulets, as well as arrangements for [[kagura]] and ritual offerings.
=== location === Adjacent to Kuratayama (倉田山) lies "Ainoyama" (間の山), home to a small [[kofun]] (ancient tomb) called Onoue-no-Goryo (尾上御陵). This tomb is traditionally believed to be the burial site of Yamatohime-no-Mikoto. In honor of this connection, the temple town at the base of 常明寺 (Jōmyō-ji) was renamed '''Yamato Town''' (倭町, Yamatomachi) in [[:en:1868|1868]] (Meiji 1). There are conflicting theories about the tomb's shape, with some identifying it as a circular tomb ([[enzuka]]) and others as a keyhole-shaped mound ([[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]).
In [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19), the residents of Ujiyamada Town formed the {{ill|Shin’enkai|ja|神苑会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin’enkai|1=Shin'enkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590780}}, a [[:en:Foundation|Foundation]] focused on preventing fires from spreading to the shrine and preserving the shrine's aesthetic and cleanliness. In [[:en:1891|1891]] (Meiji 24), the Shin’enkai established the '''Jingu Agricultural Museum''' (神宮農業館), Japan’s oldest [[industrial museum]], near the Outer Shrine. In [[:en:1905|1905]] (Meiji 38), the museum was relocated to Kuratayama.
During [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]]'s [[Imperial visit]] to Ise Jingu, road improvements were necessary to accommodate [[:en:automobiles|automobiles]]. A road passing through Kuratayama was completed in [[:en:1910|1910]] (Meiji 43) and named '''Gokō-dōri''' (御幸通り, Imperial Road). The following year, in [[:en:1911|1911]] (Meiji 44), the Shin’enkai completed the {{ill|Jingu Chokokan|ja|神宮徴古館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Chokokan|1=Jingu Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589488}}, which featured a Versailles-inspired front garden. The Chokokan included a state guest room used for resting by dignitaries such as [[:en:Emperor Taisho|Emperor Taisho]]. Further development in Kuratayama as an educational district followed the relocation of Jingu Kōgakkan (now [[:en:Kogakkan University|Kōgakkan University]]) to the area in [[:en:1919|1919]] (Taisho 8).
Due to the proximity of Onoue-no-Goryo, Yamatohime-no-Miya was constructed on a cleared section of Kuratayama. In [[:en:1928|1928]] (Showa 3), the tomb was officially designated as the '''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Burial Reference Site''' (倭姫命御陵墓参考地) and placed under the management of the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]]. == History == [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] is credited with establishing the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], creating rituals, and setting the foundation of [[Shinto]] practices. However, no shrine dedicated to her existed before the Meiji period.
Calls for a shrine arose around 1887 (Meiji 20), culminating in its official approval in 1912 (Taishō 1). The shrine was founded as a subsidiary of the Inner Shrine in 1923 (Taishō 12), following national approval in 1919 (Taishō 8).
During the 1945 (Shōwa 20) Ujiyamada air raids, the shukueiya was burned, but the main structure remained intact.
== deity == *Main deity: [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] (やまとひめのみこと)
== Grounds ==
Main Hall: Modeled after the Inner Shrine with [[:en:Chigi|Chigi]] and [[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]], featuring a [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architectural style with thatched roofing and facing south.
Shukueiya: Provides blessings and talismans.
== Festivals == Festivals follow the Inner Shrine’s schedule, including:
[[Saitan-sai]] (January 1): Celebrating the new year and imperial lineage.
[[Kigensetsu]] (February 11): Commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s founding of the nation.
[[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] (February 20): Praying for bountiful harvests.
[[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (October 24-25): Offering the year’s harvest to Amaterasu.
[[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] (November 26): Thanking the gods for the harvest.
== Worshipers == The Yamato-hime Shrine Reverence Association, founded in 1948 (Shōwa 23), holds regular events, including biannual festivals in May and November.
== Access == *Nearest station: [[:en:Kintetsu|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Toba Line|Toba Line]], [[:en:Isuzugawa Station|Isuzugawa Station]], 10 minutes on foot. *Parking: Available at the adjacent Jingu Chōkokan museum.
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also ==
* [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] * [[Ise Grand Shrine]] * [[List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|List of 125 Shrines of Ise]] * [[Moto-Ise Shrines]]
== External Links ==

It is a subsidiary shrine ("betsugu") of the [[Kōtai Jingū|Inner Shrine]] of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]].
== Overview == [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_02.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii gate at the Omotesando entrance]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_03.JPG|thumb|200px|Chōzuya (purification fountain)]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_04.JPG|thumb|200px|Stone steps along the approach]] In [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]], there are 14 [[:en:Betsugu|Betsugu]] (associated shrines) in addition to the [[Kōtai Jingū|Inner Shrine]] (Naiku) and the [[Kōtai Jingū|Outer Shrine]] (Geku). These include {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月讀宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|2=es|3=Tsukiyomi no miya (Naikū)|4=ja|5=月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyominomiya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyominomiya|qq=}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takihara-no-Miya|qq=}}, [[:en:Izawa-no-miya|Izawa-no-Miya]], {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|lt=Kazahinomi-no-Miya|lt=Kazebi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazebi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}}, and others. Among them, the creation date is clearly documented only for Yamatohime-no-Miya (倭姫宮), which was established on [[November 5, 1923]] (Taisho 12), making it the newest of the shrines.
The enshrined deity is [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]], the fourth imperial princess of the 11th emperor, [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], as told in [[Japanese mythology]].
Like other outlying Betsugu shrines, the site has a Shukueiya (宿衛屋), a lodging for [[:en:Shinto priest|Shinto priest]]s who are stationed during visiting hours. They manage the distribution of talismans and amulets, as well as arrangements for [[kagura]] and ritual offerings.
=== location === Adjacent to Kuratayama (倉田山) lies "Ainoyama" (間の山), home to a small [[kofun]] (ancient tomb) called Onoue-no-Goryo (尾上御陵). This tomb is traditionally believed to be the burial site of Yamatohime-no-Mikoto. In honor of this connection, the temple town at the base of 常明寺 (Jōmyō-ji) was renamed '''Yamato Town''' (倭町, Yamatomachi) in [[:en:1868|1868]] (Meiji 1). There are conflicting theories about the tomb's shape, with some identifying it as a circular tomb ([[enzuka]]) and others as a keyhole-shaped mound ([[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]).
In [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19), the residents of Ujiyamada Town formed the {{ill|Shin’enkai|ja|神苑会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin’enkai|1=Shin'enkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590780}}, a [[:en:Foundation|Foundation]] focused on preventing fires from spreading to the shrine and preserving the shrine's aesthetic and cleanliness. In [[:en:1891|1891]] (Meiji 24), the Shin’enkai established the '''Jingu Agricultural Museum''' (神宮農業館), Japan’s oldest [[industrial museum]], near the Outer Shrine. In [[:en:1905|1905]] (Meiji 38), the museum was relocated to Kuratayama.
During [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]]'s [[Imperial visit]] to Ise Jingu, road improvements were necessary to accommodate [[:en:automobiles|automobiles]]. A road passing through Kuratayama was completed in [[:en:1910|1910]] (Meiji 43) and named '''Gokō-dōri''' (御幸通り, Imperial Road). The following year, in [[:en:1911|1911]] (Meiji 44), the Shin’enkai completed the {{ill|Jingu Chokokan|ja|神宮徴古館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Chokokan|1=Jingu Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589488}}, which featured a Versailles-inspired front garden. The Chokokan included a state guest room used for resting by dignitaries such as [[:en:Emperor Taisho|Emperor Taisho]]. Further development in Kuratayama as an educational district followed the relocation of Jingu Kōgakkan (now [[:en:Kogakkan University|Kōgakkan University]]) to the area in [[:en:1919|1919]] (Taisho 8).
Due to the proximity of Onoue-no-Goryo, Yamatohime-no-Miya was constructed on a cleared section of Kuratayama. In [[:en:1928|1928]] (Showa 3), the tomb was officially designated as the '''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Burial Reference Site''' (倭姫命御陵墓参考地) and placed under the management of the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]]. == History == [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] is credited with establishing the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], creating rituals, and setting the foundation of [[Shinto]] practices. However, no shrine dedicated to her existed before the Meiji period.
Calls for a shrine arose around 1887 (Meiji 20), culminating in its official approval in 1912 (Taishō 1). The shrine was founded as a subsidiary of the Inner Shrine in 1923 (Taishō 12), following national approval in 1919 (Taishō 8).
During the 1945 (Shōwa 20) Ujiyamada air raids, the shukueiya was burned, but the main structure remained intact.
== deity == *Main deity: [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] (やまとひめのみこと)
== Grounds ==
Main Hall: Modeled after the Inner Shrine with [[:en:Chigi|Chigi]] and [[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]], featuring a [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architectural style with thatched roofing and facing south.
Shukueiya: Provides blessings and talismans.
== Festivals == Festivals follow the Inner Shrine’s schedule, including:
[[Saitan-sai]] (January 1): Celebrating the new year and imperial lineage.
[[Kigensetsu]] (February 11): Commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s founding of the nation.
[[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] (February 20): Praying for bountiful harvests.
[[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (October 24-25): Offering the year’s harvest to Amaterasu.
[[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] (November 26): Thanking the gods for the harvest.
== Worshipers == The Yamato-hime Shrine Reverence Association, founded in 1948 (Shōwa 23), holds regular events, including biannual festivals in May and November.
== Access == *Nearest station: [[:en:Kintetsu|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Toba Line|Toba Line]], [[:en:Isuzugawa Station|Isuzugawa Station]], 10 minutes on foot. *Parking: Available at the adjacent Jingu Chōkokan museum.

In [[:en:1891|1891]] (Meiji 24), the Shin’enkai established the '''Jingu Agricultural Museum''' (神宮農業館), Japan’s oldest [[industrial museum]], near the Outer Shrine. In [[:en:1905|1905]] (Meiji 38), the museum was relocated to Kuratayama.
During [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]]'s [[Imperial visit]] to Ise Jingu, road improvements were necessary to accommodate [[:en:automobiles|automobiles]]. A road passing through Kuratayama was completed in [[:en:1910|1910]] (Meiji 43) and named '''Gokō-dōri''' (御幸通り, Imperial Road). The following year, in [[:en:1911|1911]] (Meiji 44), the Shin’enkai completed the {{ill|Jingu Chokokan|ja|神宮徴古館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Chokokan|1=Jingu Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589488}}, which featured a Versailles-inspired front garden. The Chokokan included a state guest room used for resting by dignitaries such as [[:en:Emperor Taisho|Emperor Taisho]]. Further development in Kuratayama as an educational district followed the relocation of Jingu Kōgakkan (now [[:en:Kogakkan University|Kōgakkan University]]) to the area in [[:en:1919|1919]] (Taisho 8).
Due to the proximity of Onoue-no-Goryo, Yamatohime-no-Miya was constructed on a cleared section of Kuratayama. In [[:en:1928|1928]] (Showa 3), the tomb was officially designated as the '''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Burial Reference Site''' (倭姫命御陵墓参考地) and placed under the management of the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]]. == History == [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] is credited with establishing the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], creating rituals, and setting the foundation of [[Shinto]] practices. However, no shrine dedicated to her existed before the Meiji period.
Calls for a shrine arose around 1887 (Meiji 20), culminating in its official approval in 1912 (Taishō 1). The shrine was founded as a subsidiary of the Inner Shrine in 1923 (Taishō 12), following national approval in 1919 (Taishō 8).
During the 1945 (Shōwa 20) Ujiyamada air raids, the shukueiya was burned, but the main structure remained intact.
== deity == *Main deity: [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] (やまとひめのみこと)
== Grounds ==
Main Hall: Modeled after the Inner Shrine with [[:en:Chigi|Chigi]] and [[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]], featuring a [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architectural style with thatched roofing and facing south.
Shukueiya: Provides blessings and talismans.
== Festivals == Festivals follow the Inner Shrine’s schedule, including:
[[Saitan-sai]] (January 1): Celebrating the new year and imperial lineage.
[[Kigensetsu]] (February 11): Commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s founding of the nation.
[[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] (February 20): Praying for bountiful harvests.
[[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (October 24-25): Offering the year’s harvest to Amaterasu.
[[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] (November 26): Thanking the gods for the harvest.
== Worshipers == The Yamato-hime Shrine Reverence Association, founded in 1948 (Shōwa 23), holds regular events, including biannual festivals in May and November.
== Access == *Nearest station: [[:en:Kintetsu|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Toba Line|Toba Line]], [[:en:Isuzugawa Station|Isuzugawa Station]], 10 minutes on foot. *Parking: Available at the adjacent Jingu Chōkokan museum.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Yamatohime-no-miya (Naiku)]][[ja:倭姫宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11387081]] Wikidata name: Yamatohime-no-miya [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Yamatohime-no-miya 01.JPG | name=Yamatohime-no-miya | location=[[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], Kusube-cho, Akaidani 5[http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/shrine/area09.php "Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan | 125 Shrines 5"] (Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan, accessed May 3, 2015) | deity={{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732}} | Rank=Betto Shrine of the [[Kōdaijingū|Inner Shrine]] | Foundation=[[1923]] (Taishō 12) | Main Structure=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates={{Coord|34.48583|N|136.72306|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Festival= | prefecture={{ill|Ise|WD=Q328067}} | architecture_style={{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545}} }} '''Yamatohime-no-miya''' (倭姫宮) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], Kusube-cho. It is a subsidiary shrine ("betsugu") of the [[Kōtai Jingū|Inner Shrine]] of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]].
== Overview == [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_02.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii gate at the Omotesando entrance]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_03.JPG|thumb|200px|Chōzuya (purification fountain)]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_04.JPG|thumb|200px|Stone steps along the approach]] In [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]], there are 14 [[:en:Betsugu|Betsugu]] (associated shrines) in addition to the [[Kōtai Jingū|Inner Shrine]] (Naiku) and the [[Kōtai Jingū|Outer Shrine]] (Geku). These include {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月讀宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|2=es|3=Tsukiyomi no miya (Naikū)|4=ja|5=月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyominomiya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyominomiya|qq=}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takihara-no-Miya|qq=}}, [[:en:Izawa-no-miya|Izawa-no-Miya]], {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|lt=Kazahinomi-no-Miya|lt=Kazebi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazebi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}}, and others. Among them, the creation date is clearly documented only for Yamatohime-no-Miya (倭姫宮), which was established on [[November 5, 1923]] (Taisho 12), making it the newest of the shrines.
The enshrined deity is [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]], the fourth imperial princess of the 11th emperor, [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], as told in [[Japanese mythology]].
Like other outlying Betsugu shrines, the site has a Shukueiya (宿衛屋), a lodging for [[:en:Shinto priest|Shinto priest]]s who are stationed during visiting hours. They manage the distribution of talismans and amulets, as well as arrangements for [[kagura]] and ritual offerings.
=== location === Adjacent to Kuratayama (倉田山) lies "Ainoyama" (間の山), home to a small [[kofun]] (ancient tomb) called Onoue-no-Goryo (尾上御陵). This tomb is traditionally believed to be the burial site of Yamatohime-no-Mikoto. In honor of this connection, the temple town at the base of 常明寺 (Jōmyō-ji) was renamed '''Yamato Town''' (倭町, Yamatomachi) in [[:en:1868|1868]] (Meiji 1). There are conflicting theories about the tomb's shape, with some identifying it as a circular tomb ([[enzuka]]) and others as a keyhole-shaped mound ([[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]).
In [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19), the residents of Ujiyamada Town formed the {{ill|Shin’enkai|ja|神苑会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin’enkai|1=Shin'enkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590780}}, a [[:en:Foundation|Foundation]] focused on preventing fires from spreading to the shrine and preserving the shrine's aesthetic and cleanliness. In [[:en:1891|1891]] (Meiji 24), the Shin’enkai established the '''Jingu Agricultural Museum''' (神宮農業館), Japan’s oldest [[industrial museum]], near the Outer Shrine. In [[:en:1905|1905]] (Meiji 38), the museum was relocated to Kuratayama.
During [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]]'s [[Imperial visit]] to Ise Jingu, road improvements were necessary to accommodate [[:en:automobiles|automobiles]]. A road passing through Kuratayama was completed in [[:en:1910|1910]] (Meiji 43) and named '''Gokō-dōri''' (御幸通り, Imperial Road). The following year, in [[:en:1911|1911]] (Meiji 44), the Shin’enkai completed the {{ill|Jingu Chokokan|ja|神宮徴古館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Chokokan|1=Jingu Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589488}}, which featured a Versailles-inspired front garden. The Chokokan included a state guest room used for resting by dignitaries such as [[:en:Emperor Taisho|Emperor Taisho]]. Further development in Kuratayama as an educational district followed the relocation of Jingu Kōgakkan (now [[:en:Kogakkan University|Kōgakkan University]]) to the area in [[:en:1919|1919]] (Taisho 8).
Due to the proximity of Onoue-no-Goryo, Yamatohime-no-Miya was constructed on a cleared section of Kuratayama. In [[:en:1928|1928]] (Showa 3), the tomb was officially designated as the '''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Burial Reference Site''' (倭姫命御陵墓参考地) and placed under the management of the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]]. == History == [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] is credited with establishing the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], creating rituals, and setting the foundation of [[Shinto]] practices. However, no shrine dedicated to her existed before the Meiji period.
Calls for a shrine arose around 1887 (Meiji 20), culminating in its official approval in 1912 (Taishō 1). The shrine was founded as a subsidiary of the Inner Shrine in 1923 (Taishō 12), following national approval in 1919 (Taishō 8).
During the 1945 (Shōwa 20) Ujiyamada air raids, the shukueiya was burned, but the main structure remained intact.
== deity == *Main deity: [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] (やまとひめのみこと)
== Grounds ==
Main Hall: Modeled after the Inner Shrine with [[:en:Chigi|Chigi]] and [[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]], featuring a [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architectural style with thatched roofing and facing south.
Shukueiya: Provides blessings and talismans.
== Festivals == Festivals follow the Inner Shrine’s schedule, including:
[[Saitan-sai]] (January 1): Celebrating the new year and imperial lineage.
[[Kigensetsu]] (February 11): Commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s founding of the nation.
[[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] (February 20): Praying for bountiful harvests.
[[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (October 24-25): Offering the year’s harvest to Amaterasu.
[[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] (November 26): Thanking the gods for the harvest.
== Worshipers == The Yamato-hime Shrine Reverence Association, founded in 1948 (Shōwa 23), holds regular events, including biannual festivals in May and November.
== Access == *Nearest station: [[:en:Kintetsu|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Toba Line|Toba Line]], [[:en:Isuzugawa Station|Isuzugawa Station]], 10 minutes on foot. *Parking: Available at the adjacent Jingu Chōkokan museum.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Yamatohime-no-miya (Naiku)]][[ja:倭姫宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11387081]] Wikidata name: Yamatohime-no-miya [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Yamatohime-no-miya 01.JPG | name=Yamatohime-no-miya | location=[[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], Kusube-cho, Akaidani 5[http://www.sengukan.jp/wp-content/themes/sengukan/shrine/area09.php "Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan | 125 Shrines 5"] (Shikinen Sengu Memorial Sengukan, accessed May 3, 2015) | deity={{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|WD=Q4388732}} | Rank=Betto Shrine of the [[Kōdaijingū|Inner Shrine]] | Foundation=[[1923]] (Taishō 12) | Main Structure=[[Shinmei-zukuri]] | coordinates={{Coord|34.48583|N|136.72306|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Festival= | prefecture={{ill|Ise|WD=Q328067}} | architecture_style={{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|WD=Q930545}} }} '''Yamatohime-no-miya''' (倭姫宮) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]], [[:en:Ise City|Ise City]], Kusube-cho. It is a subsidiary shrine ("betsugu") of the [[Kōtai Jingū|Inner Shrine]] of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]].
== Overview == [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_02.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii gate at the Omotesando entrance]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_03.JPG|thumb|200px|Chōzuya (purification fountain)]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_04.JPG|thumb|200px|Stone steps along the approach]] In [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]], there are 14 [[:en:Betsugu|Betsugu]] (associated shrines) in addition to the [[Kōtai Jingū|Inner Shrine]] (Naiku) and the [[Kōtai Jingū|Outer Shrine]] (Geku). These include {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月讀宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|2=es|3=Tsukiyomi no miya (Naikū)|4=ja|5=月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyominomiya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyominomiya|qq=}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takihara-no-Miya|qq=}}, [[:en:Izawa-no-miya|Izawa-no-Miya]], {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|lt=Kazahinomi-no-Miya|lt=Kazebi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazebi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}}, and others. Among them, the creation date is clearly documented only for Yamatohime-no-Miya (倭姫宮), which was established on [[November 5, 1923]] (Taisho 12), making it the newest of the shrines.
The enshrined deity is [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]], the fourth imperial princess of the 11th emperor, [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], as told in [[Japanese mythology]].
Like other outlying Betsugu shrines, the site has a Shukueiya (宿衛屋), a lodging for [[:en:Shinto priest|Shinto priest]]s who are stationed during visiting hours. They manage the distribution of talismans and amulets, as well as arrangements for [[kagura]] and ritual offerings.
=== location === Adjacent to Kuratayama (倉田山) lies "Ainoyama" (間の山), home to a small [[kofun]] (ancient tomb) called Onoue-no-Goryo (尾上御陵). This tomb is traditionally believed to be the burial site of Yamatohime-no-Mikoto. In honor of this connection, the temple town at the base of 常明寺 (Jōmyō-ji) was renamed '''Yamato Town''' (倭町, Yamatomachi) in [[:en:1868|1868]] (Meiji 1). There are conflicting theories about the tomb's shape, with some identifying it as a circular tomb ([[enzuka]]) and others as a keyhole-shaped mound ([[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]).
In [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19), the residents of Ujiyamada Town formed the {{ill|Shin’enkai|ja|神苑会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin’enkai|1=Shin'enkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590780}}, a [[:en:Foundation|Foundation]] focused on preventing fires from spreading to the shrine and preserving the shrine's aesthetic and cleanliness. In [[:en:1891|1891]] (Meiji 24), the Shin’enkai established the '''Jingu Agricultural Museum''' (神宮農業館), Japan’s oldest [[industrial museum]], near the Outer Shrine. In [[:en:1905|1905]] (Meiji 38), the museum was relocated to Kuratayama.
During [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]]'s [[Imperial visit]] to Ise Jingu, road improvements were necessary to accommodate [[:en:automobiles|automobiles]]. A road passing through Kuratayama was completed in [[:en:1910|1910]] (Meiji 43) and named '''Gokō-dōri''' (御幸通り, Imperial Road). The following year, in [[:en:1911|1911]] (Meiji 44), the Shin’enkai completed the {{ill|Jingu Chokokan|ja|神宮徴古館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Chokokan|1=Jingu Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589488}}, which featured a Versailles-inspired front garden. The Chokokan included a state guest room used for resting by dignitaries such as [[:en:Emperor Taisho|Emperor Taisho]]. Further development in Kuratayama as an educational district followed the relocation of Jingu Kōgakkan (now [[:en:Kogakkan University|Kōgakkan University]]) to the area in [[:en:1919|1919]] (Taisho 8).
Due to the proximity of Onoue-no-Goryo, Yamatohime-no-Miya was constructed on a cleared section of Kuratayama. In [[:en:1928|1928]] (Showa 3), the tomb was officially designated as the '''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Burial Reference Site''' (倭姫命御陵墓参考地) and placed under the management of the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]]. == History == [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] is credited with establishing the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], creating rituals, and setting the foundation of [[Shinto]] practices. However, no shrine dedicated to her existed before the Meiji period.
Calls for a shrine arose around 1887 (Meiji 20), culminating in its official approval in 1912 (Taishō 1). The shrine was founded as a subsidiary of the Inner Shrine in 1923 (Taishō 12), following national approval in 1919 (Taishō 8).
During the 1945 (Shōwa 20) Ujiyamada air raids, the shukueiya was burned, but the main structure remained intact.
== deity == *Main deity: [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] (やまとひめのみこと)
== Grounds ==
Main Hall: Modeled after the Inner Shrine with [[:en:Chigi|Chigi]] and [[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]], featuring a [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architectural style with thatched roofing and facing south.
Shukueiya: Provides blessings and talismans.
== Festivals == Festivals follow the Inner Shrine’s schedule, including:
[[Saitan-sai]] (January 1): Celebrating the new year and imperial lineage.
[[Kigensetsu]] (February 11): Commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s founding of the nation.
[[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] (February 20): Praying for bountiful harvests.
[[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (October 24-25): Offering the year’s harvest to Amaterasu.
[[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] (November 26): Thanking the gods for the harvest.
== Worshipers == The Yamato-hime Shrine Reverence Association, founded in 1948 (Shōwa 23), holds regular events, including biannual festivals in May and November.
== Access == *Nearest station: [[:en:Kintetsu|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Toba Line|Toba Line]], [[:en:Isuzugawa Station|Isuzugawa Station]], 10 minutes on foot. *Parking: Available at the adjacent Jingu Chōkokan museum.

It is a subsidiary shrine ("betsugu") of the [[Kōtai Jingū|Inner Shrine]] of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]].
== Overview == [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_02.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii gate at the Omotesando entrance]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_03.JPG|thumb|200px|Chōzuya (purification fountain)]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_04.JPG|thumb|200px|Stone steps along the approach]] In [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]], there are 14 [[:en:Betsugu|Betsugu]] (associated shrines) in addition to the [[Kōtai Jingū|Inner Shrine]] (Naiku) and the [[Kōtai Jingū|Outer Shrine]] (Geku). These include {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月讀宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|2=es|3=Tsukiyomi no miya (Naikū)|4=ja|5=月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naiku)|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyominomiya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyominomiya|qq=}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takihara-no-Miya|qq=}}, [[:en:Izawa-no-miya|Izawa-no-Miya]], {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|lt=Kazahinomi-no-Miya|lt=Kazebi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazebi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}}, and others. Among them, the creation date is clearly documented only for Yamatohime-no-Miya (倭姫宮), which was established on [[November 5, 1923]] (Taisho 12), making it the newest of the shrines.
The enshrined deity is [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]], the fourth imperial princess of the 11th emperor, [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], as told in [[Japanese mythology]].
Like other outlying Betsugu shrines, the site has a Shukueiya (宿衛屋), a lodging for [[:en:Shinto priest|Shinto priest]]s who are stationed during visiting hours. They manage the distribution of talismans and amulets, as well as arrangements for [[kagura]] and ritual offerings.
=== location === Adjacent to Kuratayama (倉田山) lies "Ainoyama" (間の山), home to a small [[kofun]] (ancient tomb) called Onoue-no-Goryo (尾上御陵). This tomb is traditionally believed to be the burial site of Yamatohime-no-Mikoto. In honor of this connection, the temple town at the base of 常明寺 (Jōmyō-ji) was renamed '''Yamato Town''' (倭町, Yamatomachi) in [[:en:1868|1868]] (Meiji 1). There are conflicting theories about the tomb's shape, with some identifying it as a circular tomb ([[enzuka]]) and others as a keyhole-shaped mound ([[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]).
In [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19), the residents of Ujiyamada Town formed the {{ill|Shin’enkai|ja|神苑会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin’enkai|1=Shin'enkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590780}}, a [[:en:Foundation|Foundation]] focused on preventing fires from spreading to the shrine and preserving the shrine's aesthetic and cleanliness. In [[:en:1891|1891]] (Meiji 24), the Shin’enkai established the '''Jingu Agricultural Museum''' (神宮農業館), Japan’s oldest [[industrial museum]], near the Outer Shrine. In [[:en:1905|1905]] (Meiji 38), the museum was relocated to Kuratayama.
During [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]]'s [[Imperial visit]] to Ise Jingu, road improvements were necessary to accommodate [[:en:automobiles|automobiles]]. A road passing through Kuratayama was completed in [[:en:1910|1910]] (Meiji 43) and named '''Gokō-dōri''' (御幸通り, Imperial Road). The following year, in [[:en:1911|1911]] (Meiji 44), the Shin’enkai completed the {{ill|Jingu Chokokan|ja|神宮徴古館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Chokokan|1=Jingu Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589488}}, which featured a Versailles-inspired front garden. The Chokokan included a state guest room used for resting by dignitaries such as [[:en:Emperor Taisho|Emperor Taisho]]. Further development in Kuratayama as an educational district followed the relocation of Jingu Kōgakkan (now [[:en:Kogakkan University|Kōgakkan University]]) to the area in [[:en:1919|1919]] (Taisho 8).
Due to the proximity of Onoue-no-Goryo, Yamatohime-no-Miya was constructed on a cleared section of Kuratayama. In [[:en:1928|1928]] (Showa 3), the tomb was officially designated as the '''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Burial Reference Site''' (倭姫命御陵墓参考地) and placed under the management of the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]]. == History == [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] is credited with establishing the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], creating rituals, and setting the foundation of [[Shinto]] practices. However, no shrine dedicated to her existed before the Meiji period.
Calls for a shrine arose around 1887 (Meiji 20), culminating in its official approval in 1912 (Taishō 1). The shrine was founded as a subsidiary of the Inner Shrine in 1923 (Taishō 12), following national approval in 1919 (Taishō 8).
During the 1945 (Shōwa 20) Ujiyamada air raids, the shukueiya was burned, but the main structure remained intact.

Main Hall: Modeled after the Inner Shrine with [[:en:Chigi|Chigi]] and [[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]], featuring a [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architectural style with thatched roofing and facing south.
Shukueiya: Provides blessings and talismans.

[[Saitan-sai]] (January 1): Celebrating the new year and imperial lineage.
[[Kigensetsu]] (February 11): Commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s founding of the nation.
[[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] (February 20): Praying for bountiful harvests.
[[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (October 24-25): Offering the year’s harvest to Amaterasu.

It is a subsidiary shrine ("betsugu") of the [[Kōtai Jingū|Inner Shrine]] of the [[Ise Grand Shrine]].
== Overview == [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_02.JPG|thumb|200px|Torii gate at the Omotesando entrance]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_03.JPG|thumb|200px|Chōzuya (purification fountain)]] [[image:Yamatohime-no-miya_04.JPG|thumb|200px|Stone steps along the approach]] In [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingu]], there are 14 [[:en:Betsugu|Betsugu]] (associated shrines) in addition to the [[Kōtai Jingū|Inner Shrine]] (Naiku) and the [[Kōtai Jingū|Outer Shrine]] (Geku). These include {{ill|Aramatsuri-no-Miya|ja|荒祭宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aramatsuri-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Taka-no-Miya|ja|多賀宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taka-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|ja|月讀宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naikū)|2=es|3=Tsukiyomi no miya (Naikū)|4=ja|5=月讀宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Naikū)|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsuchi-no-Miya|ja|土宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Tsukiyominomiya|ja|月夜見宮|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|8=ja|Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|1=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya (Geku)|2=ja|3=月夜見宮|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|lt=Tsukiyomi-no-Miya|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyominomiya|qq=}}, {{ill|Takihara-no-Miya|ja|瀧原宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takihara-no-Miya|qq=}}, [[:en:Izawa-no-miya|Izawa-no-Miya]], {{ill|Kazahinomi-no-Miya|lt=Kazahinomi-no-Miya|lt=Kazebi-no-Miya|ja|風日祈宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazebi-no-Miya|qq=}}, {{ill|Kaze-no-Miya|ja|風宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaze-no-Miya|qq=}}, and others. Among them, the creation date is clearly documented only for Yamatohime-no-Miya (倭姫宮), which was established on [[November 5, 1923]] (Taisho 12), making it the newest of the shrines.
The enshrined deity is [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]], the fourth imperial princess of the 11th emperor, [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]], as told in [[Japanese mythology]].
Like other outlying Betsugu shrines, the site has a Shukueiya (宿衛屋), a lodging for [[:en:Shinto priest|Shinto priest]]s who are stationed during visiting hours. They manage the distribution of talismans and amulets, as well as arrangements for [[kagura]] and ritual offerings.
=== location === Adjacent to Kuratayama (倉田山) lies "Ainoyama" (間の山), home to a small [[kofun]] (ancient tomb) called Onoue-no-Goryo (尾上御陵). This tomb is traditionally believed to be the burial site of Yamatohime-no-Mikoto. In honor of this connection, the temple town at the base of 常明寺 (Jōmyō-ji) was renamed '''Yamato Town''' (倭町, Yamatomachi) in [[:en:1868|1868]] (Meiji 1). There are conflicting theories about the tomb's shape, with some identifying it as a circular tomb ([[enzuka]]) and others as a keyhole-shaped mound ([[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]]).
In [[:en:1886|1886]] (Meiji 19), the residents of Ujiyamada Town formed the {{ill|Shin’enkai|ja|神苑会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin’enkai|1=Shin'enkai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590780}}, a [[:en:Foundation|Foundation]] focused on preventing fires from spreading to the shrine and preserving the shrine's aesthetic and cleanliness. In [[:en:1891|1891]] (Meiji 24), the Shin’enkai established the '''Jingu Agricultural Museum''' (神宮農業館), Japan’s oldest [[industrial museum]], near the Outer Shrine. In [[:en:1905|1905]] (Meiji 38), the museum was relocated to Kuratayama.
During [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]]'s [[Imperial visit]] to Ise Jingu, road improvements were necessary to accommodate [[:en:automobiles|automobiles]]. A road passing through Kuratayama was completed in [[:en:1910|1910]] (Meiji 43) and named '''Gokō-dōri''' (御幸通り, Imperial Road). The following year, in [[:en:1911|1911]] (Meiji 44), the Shin’enkai completed the {{ill|Jingu Chokokan|ja|神宮徴古館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingu Chokokan|1=Jingu Museum|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11589488}}, which featured a Versailles-inspired front garden. The Chokokan included a state guest room used for resting by dignitaries such as [[:en:Emperor Taisho|Emperor Taisho]]. Further development in Kuratayama as an educational district followed the relocation of Jingu Kōgakkan (now [[:en:Kogakkan University|Kōgakkan University]]) to the area in [[:en:1919|1919]] (Taisho 8).
Due to the proximity of Onoue-no-Goryo, Yamatohime-no-Miya was constructed on a cleared section of Kuratayama. In [[:en:1928|1928]] (Showa 3), the tomb was officially designated as the '''Yamatohime-no-Mikoto Burial Reference Site''' (倭姫命御陵墓参考地) and placed under the management of the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]]. == History == [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] is credited with establishing the [[Ise Grand Shrine]], creating rituals, and setting the foundation of [[Shinto]] practices. However, no shrine dedicated to her existed before the Meiji period.
Calls for a shrine arose around 1887 (Meiji 20), culminating in its official approval in 1912 (Taishō 1). The shrine was founded as a subsidiary of the Inner Shrine in 1923 (Taishō 12), following national approval in 1919 (Taishō 8).
During the 1945 (Shōwa 20) Ujiyamada air raids, the shukueiya was burned, but the main structure remained intact.
== deity == *Main deity: [[:en:Yamatohime-no-mikoto|Yamatohime-no-mikoto]] (やまとひめのみこと)
== Grounds ==
Main Hall: Modeled after the Inner Shrine with [[:en:Chigi|Chigi]] and [[:en:Katsuogi|Katsuogi]], featuring a [[Shinmei-zukuri]] architectural style with thatched roofing and facing south.
Shukueiya: Provides blessings and talismans.
== Festivals == Festivals follow the Inner Shrine’s schedule, including:
[[Saitan-sai]] (January 1): Celebrating the new year and imperial lineage.
[[Kigensetsu]] (February 11): Commemorating Emperor Jimmu’s founding of the nation.
[[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] (February 20): Praying for bountiful harvests.
[[Kannamesai Festival|Kannamesai]] (October 24-25): Offering the year’s harvest to Amaterasu.
[[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]] (November 26): Thanking the gods for the harvest.
== Worshipers == The Yamato-hime Shrine Reverence Association, founded in 1948 (Shōwa 23), holds regular events, including biannual festivals in May and November.
== Access == *Nearest station: [[:en:Kintetsu|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Toba Line|Toba Line]], [[:en:Isuzugawa Station|Isuzugawa Station]], 10 minutes on foot. *Parking: Available at the adjacent Jingu Chōkokan museum.
